questions.prompt: Reproduction needing two parents that combines genetic material from each.
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction requires two parents, each contributing a gamete (egg or sperm) through meiosis, producing offspring with genetic variation. First described systematically by Gregor Mendel in his 1866 pea-plant experiments, sexual reproduction underpins natural selection because it creates the variation Darwin observed. The fusion of haploid gametes (n) restores the diploid number (2n) at fertilization. On NYS Grade 7 Science assessments, students contrast sexual reproduction's variation advantage with asexual reproduction's speed advantage and identify it as the source of unique offspring genotypes.

questions.prompt: One-parent reproduction in which offspring are genetic copies of the parent.
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction involves one parent and produces genetically identical offspring (clones) through mitosis—observed by Walther Flemming in 1882. Examples include bacterial binary fission, yeast budding, hydra budding, starfish regeneration, and plant runners on strawberries. The advantage is speed and certainty in stable environments; the disadvantage is zero genetic variation, leaving populations vulnerable when conditions change. On NYS Grade 7 Science exams, students often identify bacteria dividing every 20 minutes as a textbook asexual case and explain why farmers who clone crops face greater disease risk.

questions.prompt: Form of asexual reproduction in which a parent splits into two equal cells.
questions.explanation: Binary fission is the asexual reproduction used by prokaryotes (bacteria and archaea), in which the single circular chromosome replicates and the parent cell pinches into two genetically identical daughter cells. E. coli divides every ~20 minutes under ideal conditions, allowing one cell to produce over a billion descendants in 10 hours. The process was first observed by Antonie van Leeuwenhoek in the 1670s using his hand-ground microscope lenses. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use binary fission to contrast prokaryotic reproduction with the more complex mitosis of eukaryotic cells.

questions.prompt: Advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction in a changing environment.
questions.explanation: Genetic variation—differences in DNA among individuals—is the key advantage of sexual reproduction because it provides the raw material natural selection acts on, as Charles Darwin argued in 1859. Variation arises from three meiotic events: crossing over (described by Thomas Hunt Morgan, 1911), independent assortment, and random fertilization. In a 2010 banana crisis study, Panama disease devastated genetically identical Cavendish bananas; wild banana populations with sexual reproduction survived. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments expect students to link variation to a population's ability to survive disease, climate shifts, or new predators.

questions.prompt: A characteristic passed from parents to offspring through genes.
questions.explanation: An inherited trait is a feature—eye color, blood type, leaf shape—passed from parent to offspring via genes on chromosomes. Gregor Mendel established the rules of inheritance in 1866 using 28,000 pea plants, identifying seven discrete traits (height, seed color, pod shape, etc.). Inherited traits contrast with acquired traits (a scar, learned language) which are not passed on. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments ask students to distinguish inherited traits (freckles, attached earlobes) from acquired traits (pierced ears, suntan) and identify genes as the units of inheritance.

questions.prompt: Austrian monk whose pea plant experiments established the rules of heredity.
questions.explanation: Gregor Mendel (1822-1884), an Augustinian friar at St. Thomas's Abbey in Brünn, published Experiments on Plant Hybridization in 1866, establishing three principles: the law of segregation, the law of independent assortment, and the concept of dominant/recessive alleles. He tracked seven traits in Pisum sativum (peas) through tens of thousands of crosses and used mathematical ratios (3:1 phenotype, 1:2:1 genotype) to deduce the existence of discrete inheritance units. His work was ignored until 1900 when rediscovered by Hugo de Vries. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require identifying Mendel as the founder of genetics.

questions.prompt: Alternate forms of a gene that determine traits, such as the gene for eye color.
questions.explanation: Alleles are different versions of the same gene that occupy the same locus on homologous chromosomes—Mendel called them factors in 1866; Wilhelm Johannsen coined the term allele in 1909. Humans inherit one allele from each parent. For example, ABO blood type has three alleles (I^A, I^B, i) producing four phenotypes. A pea plant might have the T allele (tall) or t allele (short). NYS Grade 7 Science assessments ask students to identify uppercase letters as dominant alleles and lowercase as recessive, and to recognize that heterozygous (Tt) offspring carry two different alleles.

questions.prompt: Trait that masks another when both alleles are present in a heterozygote.
questions.explanation: A dominant trait is expressed in a heterozygote (one dominant, one recessive allele), masking the recessive trait—Mendel demonstrated this in 1866 when all F1 pea offspring from TT x tt crosses appeared tall. Dominant alleles are written with uppercase letters (T for tall). In humans, brown eyes, free earlobes, and Huntington's disease are dominant. Dominance does not mean common—polydactyly (extra fingers) is dominant but rare. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the 3:1 phenotype ratio in monohybrid Punnett squares and require students to identify the dominant phenotype as the one visible in the heterozygote.

questions.prompt: Genetic diagram used to predict offspring genotype probabilities from parent crosses.
questions.explanation: The Punnett square was invented in 1905 by Reginald Punnett, a Cambridge geneticist working with William Bateson, to visualize Mendelian crosses. A 2x2 grid for monohybrid crosses (Tt x Tt) or 4x4 for dihybrid (TtYy x TtYy) shows all gamete combinations and their probabilities. A Tt x Tt cross yields the classic 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt genotype ratio and 3:1 phenotype ratio. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to fill in a Punnett square, count phenotype ratios, and convert results to percentages (75% dominant, 25% recessive in a heterozygous cross).

questions.prompt: Molecule shaped like a twisted ladder that stores genetic instructions.
questions.explanation: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the double-helix molecule that stores hereditary information in all known organisms. Its structure was determined by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953, building on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction Photo 51 and Erwin Chargaff's base-pairing rules. DNA consists of two antiparallel sugar-phosphate strands joined by complementary base pairs: adenine-thymine and guanine-cytosine. A human cell contains ~3 billion base pairs across 46 chromosomes. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify DNA as the carrier of genetic information and recognize the double helix shape.

questions.prompt: Segment of DNA that codes for one specific trait or protein.
questions.explanation: A gene is a segment of DNA that codes for one protein or RNA molecule and constitutes the basic unit of heredity. The term was coined by Wilhelm Johannsen in 1909, decades after Mendel's 1866 paper described the same units as factors. The Human Genome Project (1990-2003) identified ~20,000-25,000 human genes. Each gene occupies a specific locus on a chromosome. Mutations in a single gene can cause sickle-cell anemia, cystic fibrosis, or color blindness. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to define a gene, locate it on a chromosome, and link it to one trait.

questions.prompt: Threadlike structure in a cell nucleus made of tightly coiled DNA.
questions.explanation: A chromosome is a tightly coiled DNA-protein complex visible during cell division; the term was coined by Heinrich Wilhelm Waldeyer in 1888. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs) in body cells, including 22 autosome pairs and 1 sex chromosome pair (XX or XY). Walter Sutton and Theodor Boveri independently proposed the chromosome theory of inheritance in 1902-1903, linking Mendel's factors to chromosomes. Chromosome number is species-specific: dogs have 78, fruit flies 8, peas 14. NYS Grade 7 Science exams ask students to count human chromosomes and recognize them as the structures carrying genes.

questions.prompt: Four nucleotide bases of DNA, abbreviated A, T, G, and C.
questions.explanation: The four nitrogenous bases of DNA are adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C); they pair specifically A-T and G-C through hydrogen bonds, a rule established by Erwin Chargaff in 1950 and confirmed by Watson and Crick's 1953 model. A and G are purines (double-ring); T and C are pyrimidines (single-ring). The order of bases along the DNA strand encodes genetic information—the human genome is 3 billion base pairs. RNA replaces thymine with uracil (U). NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to complement a DNA strand by pairing A-T and G-C.

questions.prompt: Researchers in 1953 who proposed the double helix structure of DNA.
questions.explanation: James Watson and Francis Crick, working at Cambridge's Cavendish Laboratory, published The Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids in Nature on April 25, 1953, proposing DNA's double-helix structure. Their model relied on Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 X-ray diffraction image (taken with Raymond Gosling at King's College London) and Maurice Wilkins's data. Watson, Crick, and Wilkins shared the 1962 Nobel Prize; Franklin had died in 1958 and was ineligible. The discovery launched molecular biology and the modern biotechnology industry. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Watson and Crick with the 1953 DNA model and increasingly include Franklin's contribution.

questions.prompt: Cell division that produces two identical body cells for growth and repair.
questions.explanation: Mitosis is the cell division that produces two genetically identical diploid daughter cells from one parent cell, used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction. Walther Flemming first described and named the process in 1882, using salamander tissue and aniline dyes to stain chromosomes. The four phases—prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase—are followed by cytokinesis. A human body produces ~25 million new cells per second through mitosis. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify mitosis as the source of identical body cells and contrast it with meiosis's gamete production.

questions.prompt: Cell division that produces four gametes with half the chromosome number.
questions.explanation: Meiosis is a two-stage cell division producing four haploid gametes from one diploid parent cell, halving chromosome number from 2n to n. Oscar Hertwig observed it in sea-urchin eggs in 1876; the term was coined by J.B. Farmer and J.E.S. Moore in 1905. Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes; meiosis II separates sister chromatids. Crossing over in prophase I (described by Thomas Hunt Morgan, 1911) shuffles alleles between homologs, creating genetic variation. NYS Grade 7 Science exams ask students to recognize meiosis as gamete production and the source of genetic variation through crossing over.

questions.prompt: Number of chromosomes in a normal human body cell.
questions.explanation: Humans have 46 chromosomes in each somatic (body) cell, arranged as 23 homologous pairs—22 autosome pairs and 1 sex chromosome pair (XX female or XY male). The number was correctly determined by Joe Hin Tjio and Albert Levan in 1956; previously researchers had counted 48. Each parent contributes 23 chromosomes via meiosis. Down syndrome (trisomy 21) results in 47 chromosomes due to nondisjunction. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to recognize 46 as the diploid human number and 23 as the haploid number in gametes.

questions.prompt: Cell division phase in which chromosomes line up at the cell's equator.
questions.explanation: Metaphase is the second phase of mitosis (and meiosis) in which chromosomes align along the metaphase plate (cell's equator), attached to spindle fibers via kinetochores. Walther Flemming described and named the four phases in 1882. The spindle checkpoint ensures every chromosome is properly attached before anaphase begins—a safeguard discovered by Andrew Hoyt in 1991. Errors here cause nondisjunction, leading to aneuploidies like Down syndrome. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the four-phase mitosis sequence (PMAT: prophase-metaphase-anaphase-telophase) and ask students to identify metaphase by the equatorial chromosome arrangement.

questions.prompt: Process during meiosis in which homologous chromosomes exchange DNA segments.
questions.explanation: Crossing over (chiasma formation) occurs in prophase I of meiosis when homologous chromosomes exchange corresponding DNA segments, shuffling maternal and paternal alleles. Thomas Hunt Morgan first proposed it in 1911 using Drosophila melanogaster, and his student Alfred Sturtevant used recombination frequencies to build the first genetic map in 1913. Crossing over creates allelic combinations not present in either parent, accounting for genetic variation in offspring beyond independent assortment. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use crossing over to explain why siblings differ despite shared parents.

questions.prompt: Characteristic acquired during life that cannot be passed to offspring.
questions.explanation: An acquired trait is developed during an organism's lifetime through environment or experience—a tan, scar, learned language, calluses, or pierced ears—and is NOT encoded in DNA, so it cannot be inherited. Jean-Baptiste Lamarck's 1809 theory that acquired traits could be inherited (the giraffe stretching its neck) was disproven by August Weismann's 1889 experiment cutting mouse tails for 22 generations without producing tailless offspring. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments contrast acquired traits with inherited traits and ask students to identify scars or learned skills as non-heritable.

questions.prompt: Combined effect of inherited genes and environmental conditions on a trait.
questions.explanation: Gene-environment interaction (G x E) describes how an organism's phenotype results from both inherited DNA and environmental factors. Francis Galton coined nature vs. nurture in 1874; modern research shows both interact. Examples: identical twins (same genes) develop different heights with different nutrition; hydrangea flower color depends on the soil pH (genetic potential, environmental trigger); Siamese cats are darker where their body is cooler. The 1990-2003 Human Genome Project confirmed many traits show G x E. NYS Grade 7 Science exams ask students to explain why genetically identical plants can grow to different sizes.

questions.prompt: Identical twins share this percentage of their DNA.
questions.explanation: Identical (monozygotic) twins share ~100% of their nuclear DNA because they originate from a single fertilized egg (zygote) that splits within the first two weeks of development. Sir Francis Galton first proposed using twin studies in 1875. Despite identical DNA, twins develop differences from environmental factors—diet, exercise, illness, epigenetic changes (methylation patterns). A 2018 NASA twin study comparing astronaut Scott Kelly (one year in space) to his Earth-bound twin Mark documented gene expression changes from spaceflight. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use twin studies to demonstrate the interplay of genes and environment.

questions.prompt: Trait whose expression depends largely on the environment, like flower color in hydrangeas.
questions.explanation: Variable expression or phenotypic plasticity describes traits whose appearance depends heavily on environmental conditions despite a fixed genotype. Classic example: hydrangea sepals turn blue in acidic soils (pH below 6, where aluminum is bioavailable) and pink in alkaline soils (pH above 7). Studied by Anton Kerner von Marilaun in the 1880s. Other examples: butterfly wing color changes by temperature (Polyommatus, 1990s research), arrowhead plant leaves differ above vs underwater. NYS Grade 7 Science exams ask students to explain how the same genotype can yield different phenotypes across environments.

questions.prompt: Process by which environmental factors switch genes on or off without changing DNA.
questions.explanation: Epigenetics describes heritable changes in gene expression that do NOT alter the underlying DNA sequence. Conrad Waddington coined the term in 1942. Mechanisms include DNA methylation (adding methyl groups to silence genes), histone modification, and non-coding RNA. A landmark 2003 Dutch Hunger Winter study showed that famine experienced by mothers during 1944-45 caused metabolic gene methylation changes detectable in their children 60 years later. The 2014 Bygren Swedish famine study suggested grandparents' food access affected grandchildren's longevity. NYS Grade 7 Science exams introduce epigenetics as nurture affecting nature's expression.

questions.prompt: Variety of life in a particular ecosystem or on Earth as a whole.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity is the variety of life at three levels—genetic, species, and ecosystem—first formally defined by Walter Rosen in 1986 and popularized by Edward O. Wilson at the 1986 National Forum on BioDiversity. Earth hosts an estimated 8.7 million eukaryotic species (Mora et al., 2011), though only ~2 million are described. Tropical rainforests like the Amazon contain over 40,000 plant species. Biodiversity provides ecosystem services worth ~$125 trillion annually (Costanza 2014). NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to identify biodiversity as a measure of ecosystem health and the variety of species in a region.

questions.prompt: All members of one species living in the same area at the same time.
questions.explanation: A population is a group of individuals of the same species occupying a defined area and interbreeding. The term was applied to biology by Charles Darwin in 1859 in On the Origin of Species. Populations are measured by size, density, and distribution; they grow according to the equations developed by Pierre-Francois Verhulst (1838 logistic growth) and Thomas Malthus (1798 exponential growth). Examples: the wolf population of Yellowstone, the monarch butterfly population overwintering in Mexico (down 90% since 1996). NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to distinguish population (one species) from community (multiple species).

questions.prompt: All the populations of different species interacting in one area.
questions.explanation: A community is the assemblage of all interacting populations of different species in a given area; the term was used by Karl Mobius in 1877 for oyster bed communities (biocenosis) and by Frederic Clements in his 1916 plant community theory. A pond community might include frogs, fish, dragonflies, cattails, algae, and bacteria. Communities are characterized by species richness (number of species) and evenness (relative abundance). Food webs, competition, predation, and symbiosis describe community interactions. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify community as the multi-species level between population and ecosystem.

questions.prompt: Single species whose removal would dramatically change an ecosystem.
questions.explanation: A keystone species is one whose impact on its community far exceeds its abundance; removing it causes ecosystem collapse. Robert Paine coined the term in 1969 after his Pacific tide-pool experiments removing Pisaster ochraceus sea stars, which led to mussel domination and lost diversity. Classic examples: sea otters in kelp forests (controlling sea urchins), wolves in Yellowstone (whose 1995 reintroduction triggered a trophic cascade restoring willow, beaver, songbird populations), and beavers as ecosystem engineers building wetlands. NYS Grade 7 Science exams expect students to identify keystone species like the wolf as critical to ecosystem stability.

questions.prompt: Number of species native only to one area, a key biodiversity hotspot measure.
questions.explanation: Endemism measures species native to and found ONLY in one geographic region. Conservation biologist Norman Myers introduced biodiversity hotspots in 1988 using endemism as the key criterion—a hotspot must contain at least 1,500 endemic vascular plant species and have lost 70% of its original habitat. There are 36 such hotspots globally, including Madagascar (with lemurs found nowhere else), Hawaii (with 1,200+ endemic plant species), and California Floristic Province. The Galapagos Islands' endemic finches inspired Darwin's theory in 1835. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use endemism to explain why island ecosystems are especially vulnerable to extinction.

questions.prompt: Organism that makes its own food using sunlight, like a plant.
questions.explanation: A producer or autotroph is an organism that synthesizes its own food from inorganic sources, usually through photosynthesis (described biochemically by Melvin Calvin in 1948-1954, earning his 1961 Nobel Prize). Producers form the base of all food chains and convert solar energy into chemical energy stored in glucose: 6CO2 + 6H2O + light = C6H12O6 + 6O2. Plants, algae, and cyanobacteria are major producers. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments require students to identify producers as the first trophic level and to recognize that all consumer energy ultimately traces back to producers.

questions.prompt: Organism that eats plants for energy, like a deer or rabbit.
questions.explanation: An herbivore is a consumer that feeds exclusively on plants (producers); the term was coined by William Whewell in 1840. Herbivores occupy the second trophic level (primary consumer) in food chains. Examples include deer, rabbits, cows, grasshoppers, and elephants. Herbivores have specialized digestive systems—cows have a four-chambered stomach with microbes that break down cellulose, a strategy described by physiologist William Beaumont in 1833. Mass herbivore die-offs cascade through ecosystems; African elephant losses since 1980 (60% decline) have altered savanna structure. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test trophic level identification with herbivores as primary consumers.

questions.prompt: Diagram showing the flow of energy from one organism to the next.
questions.explanation: A food chain is a linear sequence showing energy transfer from producers through successive consumers. Charles Elton formalized the concept in his 1927 book Animal Ecology, introducing trophic levels and the pyramid of numbers. A typical chain: grass (producer) → grasshopper (primary consumer) → frog (secondary consumer) → snake (tertiary consumer) → hawk (quaternary consumer). Arrows point in the direction of energy flow. About 90% of energy is lost as heat at each level (the 10% rule, established by Raymond Lindeman in 1942), which is why food chains rarely exceed 4-5 levels. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require correct arrow direction.

questions.prompt: Approximate percentage of energy passed from one trophic level to the next.
questions.explanation: The 10% rule states that only ~10% of the energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next; the other 90% is lost as heat, used for metabolism, or unconsumed. Raymond Lindeman established this with his 1942 study of Cedar Bog Lake, Minnesota, founding ecosystem ecology. If grass stores 10,000 kcal, grasshoppers acquire ~1,000 kcal, frogs ~100 kcal, snakes ~10 kcal, and hawks ~1 kcal. This is why apex predators are rare (one hawk needs many snakes) and food chains seldom exceed five levels. NYS Grade 7 Science assessments test the 10% rule with energy pyramid calculations.

questions.prompt: Interconnected food chains in an ecosystem with multiple feeding relationships.
questions.explanation: A food web is a network of interconnected food chains showing all feeding relationships in an ecosystem—more realistic than a single chain because most organisms eat multiple species and have multiple predators. Charles Elton diagrammed the first food web for Bear Island in 1927. A salt-marsh food web (Robert Paine's 1966 Mukkaw Bay study) might include sea stars eating mussels, barnacles, snails, and chitons simultaneously. Food webs reveal trophic cascades: removing wolves from Yellowstone (pre-1995) allowed elk to overgraze willow, harming beavers and songbirds. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast linear food chains with branching food webs.

questions.prompt: Movement of water between oceans, atmosphere, land, and living things.
questions.explanation: The water cycle (hydrologic cycle) describes the continuous movement of water through Earth's spheres via evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Roman engineer Marcus Vitruvius Pollio described early versions around 25 BCE; Pierre Perrault's 1674 measurement of Seine River flow vs Paris rainfall scientifically confirmed it. Solar energy drives ~496,000 km³ of evaporation annually, and the same volume falls as precipitation. Only 2.5% of Earth's water is fresh; 99% of that is locked in ice or groundwater. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify cycle phases and label diagram arrows.

questions.prompt: Plant process that converts water and CO2 into glucose using sunlight.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis converts solar energy into chemical energy stored in glucose, with the overall equation 6CO2 + 6H2O + light → C6H12O6 + 6O2. Jan Ingenhousz discovered the role of light in 1779; Julius Sachs identified chlorophyll's location in chloroplasts in 1862; Melvin Calvin mapped the carbon-fixation pathway 1948-54 (Nobel Prize 1961). Plants release ~270 billion tons of oxygen annually—the source of nearly all atmospheric O2. Photosynthesis removes ~120 billion tons of CO2 yearly, partly offsetting fossil-fuel emissions. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require the photosynthesis equation and identification of chloroplasts as the cellular site.

questions.prompt: Cycle by which carbon moves through atmosphere, ocean, soil, and living things.
questions.explanation: The carbon cycle moves carbon between four major reservoirs: atmosphere (CO2), oceans (dissolved inorganic carbon, ~37,000 GtC), terrestrial biosphere (plants and soil, ~2,000 GtC), and lithosphere (fossil fuels and rocks). Joseph Black discovered CO2 in 1754. Carbon enters living things via photosynthesis and exits via respiration, combustion, and decomposition. Charles Keeling's continuous CO2 measurements at Mauna Loa since 1958 (the Keeling Curve) show atmospheric CO2 rising from 315 ppm to over 420 ppm by 2023. NYS Grade 7 Science exams link the carbon cycle to climate change via burning fossil fuels.

questions.prompt: Cycle that converts atmospheric N2 into usable forms by soil bacteria.
questions.explanation: The nitrogen cycle converts atmospheric N2 (78% of air) into biologically usable forms. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Rhizobium in legume root nodules, discovered by Hermann Hellriegel in 1888, and free-living Azotobacter) convert N2 to ammonia. Nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter, identified by Sergei Winogradsky in 1890) convert ammonia to nitrites then nitrates, which plants absorb. Denitrifying bacteria return N2 to the atmosphere. Fritz Haber's 1909 industrial nitrogen fixation now produces ~150 million tons of synthetic fertilizer yearly, doubling natural fixation rates. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify bacteria as essential nitrogen cycle participants.

questions.prompt: Organism that breaks down dead matter and returns nutrients to the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers—primarily bacteria and fungi—break down dead organic matter, releasing nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and carbon back into the soil for producers. Anton de Bary established the saprophytic role of fungi in the 1860s. Decomposers process ~99% of organic matter on Earth: a single gram of forest soil contains over a billion bacteria and ~50,000 fungal hyphae. Without decomposers, dead matter would accumulate and nutrients would be locked away. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast decomposers with producers and consumers and identify them as the recyclers that keep ecosystems running.

questions.prompt: Animal that hunts and eats another animal for food.
questions.explanation: A predator is an organism that kills and consumes other animals (prey) for food. Charles Darwin discussed predator-prey relationships in 1859 as drivers of natural selection. Predator-prey population dynamics follow oscillating patterns described by the Lotka-Volterra equations (Alfred Lotka 1925, Vito Volterra 1926): when prey abundant, predators thrive and reproduce, depleting prey, which causes predator decline. Classic example: lynx-snowshoe hare 10-year cycle in Canada documented by Hudson Bay Company fur records since 1845. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use predator-prey cycles to illustrate population regulation.

questions.prompt: Close long-term relationship between two different species.
questions.explanation: Symbiosis is a close, long-term biological interaction between two different species. Heinrich Anton de Bary coined the term in 1879 from Greek meaning living together. The three main types are mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, one unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, one harmed). Examples: clownfish-anemone (mutualism), barnacles on whales (commensalism), tapeworm-human (parasitism). Lynn Margulis's 1967 endosymbiotic theory proposed mitochondria and chloroplasts evolved from ancient bacterial symbioses. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to classify symbiotic relationships using +/- notation.

questions.prompt: Symbiotic relationship in which both organisms benefit, like bees and flowers.
questions.explanation: Mutualism is a symbiosis benefiting both species (+/+). Pierre-Joseph van Beneden coined the term in 1876. Examples include bees pollinating flowers (bees get nectar, flowers get gene flow), mycorrhizal fungi exchanging nitrogen for sugars with 90% of plant roots (described by A.B. Frank in 1885), and gut bacteria like Bacteroides aiding human digestion (Joshua Lederberg's microbiome research, 1958+). Cleaner fish like wrasses remove parasites from larger fish (Conrad Limbaugh, 1961). 80% of plant species depend on mutualism. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use mutualism to explain ecosystem interdependence.

questions.prompt: Symbiosis in which one organism benefits while the other is harmed.
questions.explanation: Parasitism is a symbiosis in which one organism (the parasite) benefits at the expense of another (the host), classified +/-. Italian physician Francesco Redi pioneered the study of parasites in 1668 with experiments on maggots disproving spontaneous generation. Common parasites include tapeworms (Taenia solium), ticks, fleas, head lice, and parasitic plants like mistletoe and dodder. Pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi) are microbial parasites. Theobald Smith's 1893 discovery of cattle Texas fever transmission by ticks founded vector-borne disease epidemiology. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require classifying parasite-host as +/-.

questions.prompt: Struggle between species for the same limited resource like food or space.
questions.explanation: Competition is the interaction in which two species struggle for the same limited resource (food, water, space, light), classified -/-. Russian biologist Georgy Gause formulated the competitive exclusion principle in 1934 (Gause's Law): two species competing for identical resources cannot coexist indefinitely; one will outcompete the other. His Paramecium experiments showed P. aurelia drove out P. caudatum. Joseph Connell's 1961 barnacle study on Scottish coasts demonstrated competitive exclusion in nature. Niche partitioning—different feeding heights for warblers (Robert MacArthur 1958)—allows coexistence. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use Gause's principle to explain species distributions.

questions.prompt: Maximum population size an environment can support long-term.
questions.explanation: Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size of a species that an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources. Pierre-Francois Verhulst introduced the concept mathematically in 1838 with the logistic growth equation dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K), where r is the intrinsic growth rate and K is carrying capacity. Earth's human carrying capacity is debated—Paul Ehrlich's 1968 The Population Bomb predicted 1970s collapse at 3.5 billion; we now have 8 billion. African lion habitat carrying capacity has fallen 80% since 1960. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use logistic S-curves to show population leveling off at K.

questions.prompt: Resource scarcity that limits a population's growth.
questions.explanation: A limiting factor is any resource or condition restricting population growth. Justus von Liebig formulated the Law of the Minimum in 1840 from agricultural studies: plant growth is constrained by the scarcest nutrient (like phosphorus in many soils), not the total supply. Common limiting factors: water in deserts, sunlight in dense forests, nitrogen in oceans (causing dead zones when added), space in coral reefs. Density-dependent factors (disease, competition) intensify as population grows; density-independent factors (drought, floods, fires) act regardless. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify what limits a given population.

questions.prompt: Type of population growth that produces a J-shaped curve with unlimited resources.
questions.explanation: Exponential growth occurs when population increase is proportional to current size, producing a J-shaped curve: dN/dt = rN. Thomas Malthus described it in his 1798 Essay on the Principle of Population, warning humans grow exponentially while food grows linearly. Bacteria in fresh nutrient broth or rabbits introduced to Australia in 1859 (24 rabbits ballooned to 600 million by 1950) show classic exponential growth. The pattern collapses when resources deplete. Human population grew exponentially from 1 billion in 1804 to 8 billion in 2022. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast the J-curve (exponential) with the S-curve (logistic).

questions.prompt: S-shaped growth curve produced when populations approach carrying capacity.
questions.explanation: Logistic growth produces an S-shaped curve as a population increases rapidly at first, then slows as it approaches the carrying capacity (K). Pierre-Francois Verhulst derived the equation dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K) in 1838. Raymond Pearl and Lowell Reed applied it to US census data in 1920 to predict population leveling. Classic example: Georgy Gause's 1934 Paramecium aurelia cultures grew logistically to K=375 cells/mL. Real populations often oscillate around K rather than smoothly leveling. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify the S-curve as logistic and link the plateau to carrying capacity and limiting factors.

questions.prompt: Sudden, sharp drop in population from disease, disaster, or resource loss.
questions.explanation: A population crash is a rapid, severe decline in population size, usually after a species exceeds carrying capacity or faces a catastrophic event. Classic case: 29 reindeer introduced to St. Matthew Island, Alaska in 1944 grew to 6,000 by 1963 (exceeding K), then crashed to 42 by 1966 after overgrazing lichen—David Klein's 1968 ecological study. Other examples: Atlantic cod collapse 1992 after overfishing (Newfoundland), monarch butterfly Mexican overwintering population down 90% since 1996. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use crashes to show what happens when populations exceed carrying capacity or limiting factors change suddenly.

questions.prompt: Type of rock formed from cooled magma or lava, like granite or basalt.
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks form from the cooling and solidification of magma (underground) or lava (surface). Charles Lyell formalized rock classification in his Principles of Geology (1830-33). Intrusive igneous rocks like granite cool slowly underground, growing large crystals; extrusive rocks like basalt cool quickly at the surface, with tiny crystals. The Hawaiian Islands are entirely volcanic basalt; Yosemite's Half Dome is granite. Igneous rocks make up ~95% of the upper 16 km of Earth's crust (Bowen's reaction series, 1922). NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify rocks as igneous from cooling magma or lava.

questions.prompt: Rock formed by layered deposits of sediment, often containing fossils.
questions.explanation: Sedimentary rocks form from compacted and cemented sediments—weathered fragments, organic material, or precipitated minerals. James Hutton described the cycle in 1788. Examples: sandstone (sand), limestone (calcium carbonate from shells), shale (clay), conglomerate (pebbles). The Grand Canyon exposes 2 billion years of sedimentary layers. Nicolas Steno's 1669 Law of Superposition states older layers lie below younger ones. Nearly all fossils form in sedimentary rocks because high heat would destroy organic remains. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use the presence of fossils and visible layers (strata) to identify sedimentary rocks.

questions.prompt: Rock changed by intense heat and pressure deep underground, like marble.
questions.explanation: Metamorphic rocks form when existing rocks (igneous, sedimentary, or other metamorphic) are transformed by intense heat and pressure without melting completely. George Barrow mapped metamorphic zones in Scotland in 1893, identifying mineral indicators of pressure-temperature conditions. Limestone metamorphoses into marble; shale into slate then schist then gneiss; sandstone into quartzite. The Greek-derived name means changed in form. The Taj Mahal is built of metamorphic Makrana marble (originally limestone). NYS Grade 7 Science exams test metamorphic identification by foliated banding (gneiss) or crystalline texture from non-melted recrystallization.

questions.prompt: Physical and chemical breakdown of rock at Earth's surface by wind, water, and ice.
questions.explanation: Weathering is the in-situ breakdown of rocks at or near Earth's surface by physical and chemical agents. James Hutton recognized it in 1788 as the source of sediment. Physical (mechanical) weathering includes frost wedging (water freezing/expanding in cracks), thermal expansion, and root pry. Chemical weathering—oxidation, hydrolysis, carbonation—dissolves minerals; rainwater is naturally acidic (pH ~5.6) due to dissolved CO2. The 130-meter-tall Mt. Rushmore sculpture (carved 1927-41 from granite) erodes ~2.5 cm per 10,000 years. NYS Grade 7 Science exams distinguish weathering (breakdown in place) from erosion (movement of sediment).

questions.prompt: Movement of sediment from one location to another by wind, water, or glaciers.
questions.explanation: Erosion is the transport of weathered material from one place to another by agents including running water, wind, glaciers, and gravity. William Morris Davis described the cycle of erosion in 1899. The Mississippi River carries ~210 million metric tons of sediment to the Gulf annually. The 1930s American Dust Bowl displaced 850 million tons of topsoil and forced 2.5 million people to migrate—Hugh Hammond Bennett's testimony to Congress led to the 1935 Soil Conservation Act. Glacial erosion carved Yosemite Valley's U-shape. NYS Grade 7 Science exams distinguish erosion (transport) from weathering (in-place breakdown) and deposition (settling).

questions.prompt: Preserved remains or imprint of an ancient organism, usually in sedimentary rock.
questions.explanation: A fossil is the preserved remains, imprint, or trace of an ancient organism, generally over 10,000 years old. Greek philosopher Xenophanes (~500 BCE) recognized seashells in mountains as ancient marine fossils. The science of paleontology was founded by Georges Cuvier in the early 1800s. Fossils form when organisms are rapidly buried in sediment, protecting remains from decomposers and scavengers; minerals replace original material in petrification. Famous fossils include Lucy (Australopithecus afarensis, 1974, 3.2 million years old) and Sue (Tyrannosaurus rex, 1990, 67 million years old). NYS Grade 7 Science exams require fossil identification in sedimentary rock.

questions.prompt: Scientist who studies fossils to understand ancient life.
questions.explanation: A paleontologist studies fossils to reconstruct ancient life, evolution, and Earth's biological history. Georges Cuvier founded vertebrate paleontology in the early 1800s, comparing fossil mammoth bones to modern elephants and proving extinction was real—a radical idea in 1796. Mary Anning (1799-1847) was a pioneering British fossil hunter who discovered the first complete Ichthyosaurus skeleton at Lyme Regis. Modern paleontologists like Jack Horner studied Maiasaura nest sites in Montana (1979) showing dinosaur parental care. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit paleontologists as the scientists who interpret the fossil record.

questions.prompt: Method to find the age of organic material by measuring carbon-14 decay.
questions.explanation: Radiocarbon (carbon-14) dating measures the decay of C-14 in organic remains to determine age up to ~50,000 years. Willard Libby developed the method at the University of Chicago in 1949, winning the 1960 Nobel Prize. C-14 has a half-life of 5,730 years and forms in the atmosphere from nitrogen bombarded by cosmic rays. Living things absorb C-14 constantly; at death, intake stops and C-14 decays at a known rate. Carbon dating confirmed the Dead Sea Scrolls as 2,000+ years old (1955) and the Iceman Otzi as 5,300 years old (1991). NYS Grade 7 Science exams use C-14 for organic material under 50,000 years.

questions.prompt: Geologic principle that older rock layers are usually found below younger ones.
questions.explanation: The Law of Superposition states that in undisturbed sedimentary rock sequences, older layers (strata) lie below younger ones. Danish naturalist Nicolas Steno formulated this in his 1669 De Solido (along with original horizontality and lateral continuity), founding stratigraphy. William Smith's 1815 geologic map of England applied superposition to construct the first historical rock-age sequence. The Grand Canyon's 40+ visible layers (oldest Vishnu Schist at bottom, ~1.7 billion years; youngest Kaibab Limestone on top, ~270 million years) demonstrate superposition. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to order rock layers by age using this principle.

questions.prompt: Fossil of a widespread, short-lived species used to date rock layers.
questions.explanation: An index fossil is from a species that was geographically widespread but existed only briefly geologically, making it ideal for dating rock layers worldwide. William Smith pioneered the use of fossils for correlation in 1815, working on English canal cuts. Classic index fossils include trilobites (Cambrian-Permian, 521-252 mya), ammonites (Devonian-Cretaceous), and the Foraminifera Fusulina (Pennsylvanian-Permian). When the same index fossil appears in rocks on different continents, those rocks must be the same age—a key tool for biostratigraphy. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify ideal index-fossil traits: widespread + short-lived.

questions.prompt: Theory that Earth's outer shell consists of moving plates.
questions.explanation: Plate tectonics is the unifying theory of geology stating that Earth's lithosphere is broken into ~15 major plates that move atop the plastic asthenosphere. The theory was assembled in the 1960s by John Tuzo Wilson, Harry Hess (seafloor spreading, 1962), Fred Vine and Drummond Matthews (magnetic stripe evidence, 1963), and J.T. Wilson's 1965 transform faults paper. It explains earthquakes, volcanoes, mountain building, and continental drift in one framework. Plates move 2-15 cm/year (about as fast as fingernails grow). NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify plate tectonics as the theory unifying Earth's surface processes.

questions.prompt: 1912 scientist who proposed continents had moved across Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Alfred Wegener (1880-1930), a German meteorologist, proposed continental drift in 1912 in The Origin of Continents and Oceans. He noted the matching coastlines of Africa and South America (jigsaw fit), identical fossils (Mesosaurus, Glossopteris) on separated continents, and matching rock formations and ancient climate evidence (glacial deposits in tropical regions). He hypothesized a supercontinent named Pangaea (~300 million years ago) that fragmented. Wegener was ridiculed because he couldn't explain HOW continents moved; the mechanism awaited the 1960s seafloor spreading evidence. He died on Greenland ice in 1930. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Wegener as the originator of continental drift.

questions.prompt: Boundary where two tectonic plates collide and one slides beneath the other.
questions.explanation: A convergent boundary is where two tectonic plates move toward each other; if one is oceanic, it subducts beneath the other (denser ocean crust dives under lighter continental crust). Discovered by Hugo Benioff in 1949 (Benioff zones describe inclined earthquake patterns at subduction sites). The Andes formed where the Nazca Plate subducts under South America; the Himalaya rose where India collided with Eurasia 50 million years ago and continues rising ~5 mm/year. The Pacific Ring of Fire's volcanoes and the 2011 Tohoku earthquake (9.1 magnitude) occur at convergent boundaries. NYS Grade 7 Science exams link convergent boundaries to volcanoes and mountain building.

questions.prompt: Boundary where two plates move apart, often forming a mid-ocean ridge.
questions.explanation: A divergent boundary is where two tectonic plates move away from each other, allowing magma to rise and form new crust. Harry Hess proposed seafloor spreading in 1962; Fred Vine and Drummond Matthews confirmed it with magnetic stripe symmetry in 1963. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge—a 16,000-km undersea mountain chain—is the longest divergent boundary, spreading 2.5 cm/year. Iceland, on the ridge, gains ~2 cm width annually. On land, the East African Rift is forming a future ocean basin. The Atlantic was a narrow rift 200 million years ago. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use mid-ocean ridges as the type-locality of divergence.

questions.prompt: Single supercontinent that existed about 300 million years ago.
questions.explanation: Pangaea was a supercontinent assembled from all of Earth's landmasses, existing from ~335 to ~175 million years ago during the late Paleozoic to early Mesozoic eras. Alfred Wegener coined the name in 1912 from Greek meaning all earth. Pangaea broke apart in the Jurassic into Laurasia (North America, Europe, Asia) in the north and Gondwana (South America, Africa, Antarctica, Australia, India) in the south. The Atlantic Ocean opened ~180 million years ago. Evidence includes matching fossil reptiles (Lystrosaurus, Cynognathus) and the Glossopteris fern across continents. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the name Pangaea and the breakup timeline.

questions.prompt: Calendar dividing Earth's 4.6-billion-year history into eras and periods.
questions.explanation: The Geologic Time Scale is the chronological calendar of Earth's 4.6-billion-year history, dividing time into eons, eras, periods, and epochs. William Smith's 1815 geological map of England and Adam Sedgwick and Roderick Murchison's 1830s-40s work on the Cambrian and Silurian established the basis. The International Commission on Stratigraphy updates the modern scale. Major divisions include the Precambrian (4,600-541 mya, 88% of Earth history) and the Phanerozoic Eon (541 mya-present), divided into Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and Cenozoic eras. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require recognition of major eras and the ~4.6 billion year Earth age.

questions.prompt: Era of dinosaurs, lasting from 252 to 66 million years ago.
questions.explanation: The Mesozoic Era (252-66 mya) spans the Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous periods. The name was coined by John Phillips in 1841 from Greek meaning middle life. Dinosaurs first appeared in the Late Triassic (~230 mya), diversified through the Jurassic (Stegosaurus, Brachiosaurus), and peaked in the Cretaceous (Tyrannosaurus, Triceratops). Flowering plants (angiosperms) emerged in the Cretaceous. The era ended with the K-Pg extinction (66 mya)—the Chicxulub asteroid impact in Mexico's Yucatan, identified by Luis and Walter Alvarez in 1980—killing 75% of species. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test Mesozoic as the dinosaur era.

questions.prompt: Mass extinction 66 million years ago that ended the dinosaurs.
questions.explanation: The K-Pg (formerly K-T) extinction 66 million years ago wiped out ~75% of species including all non-avian dinosaurs, marking the end of the Cretaceous and beginning of the Paleogene. Luis Alvarez (Nobel physicist) and his son Walter Alvarez (geologist) proposed in 1980 that an asteroid impact caused it, citing an iridium-rich clay layer worldwide. The 2016 Chicxulub crater drilling project confirmed the 10-12 km asteroid struck Mexico's Yucatan, releasing energy equal to 10 billion Hiroshima bombs. Birds (avian dinosaurs) survived. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the date (66 mya), name (K-Pg), and asteroid cause.

questions.prompt: Method using radioactive decay rates to date rocks billions of years old.
questions.explanation: Radiometric dating measures the decay of unstable isotopes to determine absolute rock ages. Ernest Rutherford and Frederick Soddy discovered radioactive decay in 1902; Arthur Holmes pioneered radiometric dating of rocks in 1913, eventually publishing the first geologic time scale in 1937. Common systems: uranium-lead (U-238 half-life 4.47 billion years, ideal for oldest rocks), potassium-argon, rubidium-strontium. Clair Patterson used U-Pb dating of the Canyon Diablo meteorite in 1956 to establish Earth's age at 4.55 billion years. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast radiometric dating (rocks, billions of years) with carbon-14 (organic remains, under 50,000 years).

questions.prompt: Time it takes for half of a radioactive sample to decay.
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time required for half of a sample of a radioactive isotope to decay into its daughter isotope, a constant unique to each isotope. Ernest Rutherford discovered the concept in 1907 and applied it to dating. After one half-life, 50% remains; after two, 25%; after three, 12.5%; and so on. Carbon-14's half-life is 5,730 years (used for organic remains); uranium-238 is 4.47 billion years (used for the oldest Earth rocks); potassium-40 is 1.25 billion years. Marie and Pierre Curie's 1898 discoveries of polonium and radium underlie this field. NYS Grade 7 Science exams ask students to calculate ages from given half-lives.

questions.prompt: Solid rocky outer shell of the Earth.
questions.explanation: Earth's crust is the thin, brittle outer shell averaging 5-10 km thick under oceans (oceanic crust, mostly basalt) and 30-50 km thick under continents (continental crust, mostly granite). The crust was first distinguished from deeper layers by Andrija Mohorovicic in 1909, who identified the Moho discontinuity—a sudden seismic wave speed change marking the crust-mantle boundary. The crust comprises less than 1% of Earth's volume. The thickest crust (70 km) lies under the Himalaya. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify the crust as Earth's outermost layer and to recognize oceanic vs continental crust.

questions.prompt: Thick layer of hot, semi-solid rock between the crust and outer core.
questions.explanation: The mantle is Earth's middle layer, ~2,900 km thick, composed of silicate rocks rich in iron and magnesium (peridotite). Discovered seismically by Andrija Mohorovicic in 1909, it makes up ~84% of Earth's volume. The mantle is not liquid but plastic—it flows over geologic time (centimeters per year) creating convection currents that drive plate tectonics. Arthur Holmes proposed mantle convection as the engine of continental drift in 1929-1944. The upper mantle includes the asthenosphere (~100-350 km depth) on which lithospheric plates ride. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to link mantle convection to plate motion.

questions.prompt: Layer of liquid iron and nickel between the mantle and inner core.
questions.explanation: The outer core is a 2,200-km-thick layer of liquid iron and nickel between the mantle and inner core (~2,890 to 5,150 km depth). Inge Lehmann inferred its liquid state in 1936 from S-waves (shear waves) not passing through it; only liquids cannot transmit S-waves. Temperatures range from 4,000 to 6,000 K. Convection of the liquid metallic outer core generates Earth's magnetic field via the geodynamo mechanism, first proposed by Walter Elsasser in 1939. The magnetic field shields Earth from solar wind, allowing the atmosphere to persist. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify the outer core as liquid iron/nickel and the source of Earth's magnetic field.

questions.prompt: Solid metallic center of the Earth at extremely high pressure.
questions.explanation: The inner core is a solid sphere of iron-nickel alloy ~1,220 km in radius, at the very center of Earth (5,150-6,371 km depth). Inge Lehmann discovered it in 1936 by analyzing seismic waves from a New Zealand earthquake, noticing P-waves reflecting off a sharp inner boundary. Temperatures reach ~5,400 K (similar to the Sun's surface), but pressures of 330-360 GPa keep iron solid. The inner core rotates slightly faster than the rest of Earth (super-rotation, ~0.3-0.5 degrees/year, documented by Xiaodong Song in 1996). NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify the inner core as solid iron-nickel under intense pressure.

questions.prompt: Seismic boundary discovered by Andrija Mohorovicic in 1909.
questions.explanation: The Mohorovicic discontinuity (Moho) is the seismic boundary between Earth's crust and mantle, marked by an abrupt jump in P-wave velocity from ~6 km/s to ~8 km/s. Croatian seismologist Andrija Mohorovicic discovered it in 1909 while analyzing the 1909 Kupa Valley earthquake. The Moho lies ~5-10 km below the ocean floor and 30-50 km below continents. Project Mohole (1961-66) attempted to drill through the Moho near Mexico but stopped at 183 meters into ocean basalt due to funding. The deepest borehole ever, Russia's Kola Superdeep (12.3 km, completed 1989), did not reach the Moho. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the Moho as the crust-mantle boundary.

questions.prompt: Naturally occurring inorganic solid with definite chemical composition and crystal structure.
questions.explanation: A mineral is a naturally occurring, inorganic, crystalline solid with a specific chemical composition. The International Mineralogical Association recognizes ~5,800 valid mineral species. Georg Bauer (Agricola) wrote the first systematic mineralogy text, De Re Metallica, in 1556. Common rock-forming minerals include quartz, feldspar, mica, amphibole, pyroxene, and olivine. Friedrich Mohs developed the Mohs hardness scale (1-10) in 1812—diamond is 10, talc is 1. Diamonds and graphite are both pure carbon (allotropes), but their crystal structure makes one the hardest natural substance and the other one of the softest. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the 4-part mineral definition.

questions.prompt: 1812 scale that ranks mineral hardness from talc to diamond.
questions.explanation: The Mohs Hardness Scale ranks minerals 1-10 by scratch resistance. German geologist Friedrich Mohs developed it in 1812 using 10 reference minerals: talc (1), gypsum (2), calcite (3), fluorite (4), apatite (5), feldspar/orthoclase (6), quartz (7), topaz (8), corundum (9), diamond (10). The scale is ordinal—diamond is ~4 times harder than corundum, not 10/9 times. A fingernail rates ~2.5, a copper coin ~3.5, a steel knife ~5.5, glass ~5.5. Diamond's record was held since 1812 until 2009 when ultrahard rhenium-boride and aggregated diamond nanorods were synthesized. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test scratch tests and the 1-10 scale.

questions.prompt: Mineral deposit that can be mined profitably for a metal like iron or copper.
questions.explanation: An ore is a naturally occurring rock or mineral deposit containing enough of a valuable element (usually a metal) to make extraction economically worthwhile. Georg Bauer (Agricola) systematized mining engineering in his 1556 De Re Metallica. Common ores: hematite/magnetite (iron), bauxite (aluminum), chalcopyrite (copper), galena (lead). The Mesabi Iron Range in Minnesota, opened in 1892, supplied much of the steel for American industrial growth and WWII. Aluminum—the most abundant metal in Earth's crust (8.1%)—was once more valuable than gold until the 1886 Hall-Heroult electrolysis process. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify ores as profitable mineral deposits.

questions.prompt: Test that uses streak plate to identify minerals by powder color.
questions.explanation: A streak test identifies minerals by the color of their powder when rubbed on an unglazed porcelain plate (Mohs ~7). The Mohs hardness scale (1812) made the test practical. Streak color is often diagnostic—hematite (Fe2O3) is metallic gray in solid form but produces a red-brown streak; pyrite (fool's gold) gives a black-green streak versus real gold's gold streak. Streak removes weathering effects and reveals true mineral identity. Mineralogist Friedrich Mohs included streak in his diagnostic framework. NYS Grade 7 Science exams use streak to distinguish similar-looking minerals like pyrite (greenish-black streak) from gold (yellow streak).

questions.prompt: Property of minerals to break along smooth, flat surfaces, like mica.
questions.explanation: Cleavage is the tendency of a mineral to break along smooth, flat planes determined by weaknesses in its crystal structure. The concept was formalized by Rene-Just Hauy in 1784 after he accidentally dropped a calcite crystal and noticed its consistent angles. Mica has perfect basal cleavage (peeling like pages); halite has three cleavage directions at 90 degrees; calcite has rhombohedral cleavage; quartz lacks cleavage entirely (it has conchoidal fracture instead, like glass). Diamond has four cleavage directions, allowing gem cutters to split rough stones. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast cleavage (flat planes) with fracture (irregular breaks).

questions.prompt: Solid fossil fuel formed from ancient plant material compressed over millions of years.
questions.explanation: Coal is a sedimentary fossil fuel formed from ancient plant matter compressed and heated over millions of years. The Carboniferous Period (358-298 mya) produced most of today's coal as massive swamp forests of Lepidodendron and Calamites were buried. Coal stages progress from peat to lignite to bituminous to anthracite (hardest, ~95% carbon). George Stephenson's coal-fueled locomotive (1814) launched the Industrial Revolution. Coal still generates ~36% of global electricity and ~27% of US power (2022). Burning coal releases CO2, mercury, and sulfur, contributing to climate change and acid rain. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify coal as a non-renewable, carbon-rich fossil fuel.

questions.prompt: Liquid fossil fuel refined into gasoline, jet fuel, and plastics.
questions.explanation: Petroleum is a liquid mixture of hydrocarbons formed from marine plankton and algae buried and heated in sedimentary basins over 10-300 million years. Edwin Drake drilled the first commercial oil well in Titusville, Pennsylvania, in 1859. The world consumes ~100 million barrels per day (2022). Saudi Arabia, the US, and Russia are top producers. Refining separates petroleum by boiling point into gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, heating oil, lubricants, asphalt, and feedstocks for plastics. The 2010 BP Deepwater Horizon spill released ~4.9 million barrels into the Gulf of Mexico. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify petroleum as a non-renewable fossil fuel.

questions.prompt: Gaseous fossil fuel, mostly methane, used for heating and electricity.
questions.explanation: Natural gas is a fossil fuel composed mostly of methane (CH4, 70-90%) with ethane, propane, butane, and CO2. It forms alongside petroleum from buried organic matter. Joseph Priestley first isolated methane in 1776; Alessandro Volta studied marsh gas in 1778. Modern natural gas extraction in the US tripled from 2005-2020 thanks to hydraulic fracturing (fracking) in shale formations like the Marcellus (Pennsylvania-NY-Ohio). Natural gas burns cleaner than coal—producing ~50% less CO2 per unit of energy—but methane leaks during extraction have 84x the 20-year warming impact of CO2. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify methane as the main component.

questions.prompt: Resource that cannot be replenished within a human lifetime.
questions.explanation: A nonrenewable resource is one that takes geological timeframes (millions of years) to form, far longer than humans use it. Examples include fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas), metallic minerals (iron, copper, gold), and uranium. M. King Hubbert predicted US peak oil in 1956—his curve correctly forecast US conventional oil production peaking in 1970, though fracking later created a second peak in 2018. Coal reserves are estimated at ~130 years at current consumption; conventional oil ~50 years; natural gas ~50 years. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast nonrenewable with renewable resources and link nonrenewable depletion to alternative energy needs.

questions.prompt: Greenhouse gas released when fossil fuels are burned, driving climate change.
questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide (CO2) is the primary greenhouse gas released by burning fossil fuels, deforestation, and cement production. Joseph Fourier first proposed the greenhouse effect in 1824; John Tyndall measured CO2's heat absorption in 1859; Svante Arrhenius calculated in 1896 that doubling atmospheric CO2 would warm Earth ~5-6 degrees Celsius. Charles Keeling began continuous CO2 measurements at Mauna Loa Observatory in 1958, producing the Keeling Curve. CO2 has risen from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to over 420 ppm in 2023—the highest level in at least 800,000 years per Antarctic ice cores. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require CO2 as the chief anthropogenic greenhouse gas.

questions.prompt: Energy source that uses the sun's light to generate electricity.
questions.explanation: Solar energy converts sunlight to electricity (photovoltaics, PV) or heat (solar thermal). Edmond Becquerel discovered the photovoltaic effect in 1839; Bell Labs (Calvin Fuller, Daryl Chapin, Gerald Pearson) built the first practical silicon solar cell in 1954 at 6% efficiency. Modern commercial panels reach ~22% efficiency; lab record is 47.6% (multi-junction cells, 2022). Global solar capacity grew from 1.4 GW (2000) to over 1,200 GW (2022). China alone added 216 GW in 2023. The cost of solar electricity fell ~90% from 2009 to 2022. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify solar as renewable, low-emission, and increasingly affordable.

questions.prompt: Energy source that captures moving air with large turbine blades.
questions.explanation: Wind energy converts wind kinetic energy to electricity using turbines. Charles Brush built the first electricity-generating wind turbine in Cleveland in 1887-88. Modern utility-scale turbines have blades up to 107 meters long (GE Haliade-X) and produce 14-15 MW each. Texas leads US wind production with 40,000+ MW; Iowa generates 60%+ of its electricity from wind. Denmark gets ~50% of its electricity from wind. The Gansu Wind Farm in China (~20 GW) is the world's largest. Wind energy capacity worldwide grew from 17 GW (2000) to 906 GW (2022). NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify wind as renewable and a major source of low-emission electricity.

questions.prompt: Energy from falling or flowing water, used by dams to generate electricity.
questions.explanation: Hydroelectric power generates electricity from falling water, typically via dams. The first hydroelectric plant began operating in Appleton, Wisconsin, in 1882, designed by H.F. Rogers. Today, hydropower supplies ~16% of global electricity and ~7% of US electricity (2022). The Three Gorges Dam in China (22.5 GW, completed 2012) is the world's largest power plant; the Itaipu Dam (Brazil-Paraguay, 14 GW, 1984) is second. Hydropower is renewable but disrupts river ecosystems—the US has begun removing aging dams, including Washington's Elwha Dam (2011-14) restoring salmon runs. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify hydropower as renewable but environmentally impactful.

questions.prompt: Heat energy drawn from inside the Earth, used in Iceland and Yellowstone.
questions.explanation: Geothermal energy taps Earth's internal heat for electricity or direct heating. Larderello, Italy, began geothermal electricity generation in 1911 using volcanic steam. Iceland generates ~25% of its electricity and 90% of its heating from geothermal sources, harnessing the Mid-Atlantic Ridge volcanism. The Geysers field in California is the world's largest geothermal power plant (~835 MW). Earth's internal heat (from primordial accretion and radioactive decay of uranium, thorium, and potassium) totals ~47 terawatts at the surface. Old Faithful geyser in Yellowstone erupts every 60-110 minutes. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify geothermal as renewable Earth-derived heat.

questions.prompt: Fuel made from plant material or animal waste, like ethanol from corn.
questions.explanation: Biomass is plant or animal material used for energy via combustion (wood, agricultural waste) or conversion to biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel). Wood was the dominant fuel source until coal overtook it in the 1880s. Modern biomass includes corn ethanol (US makes ~15 billion gallons/year, mostly mixed into gasoline as E10), sugarcane ethanol (Brazil's flex-fuel cars run on up to E100), and biodiesel from soy or canola. Biomass is theoretically renewable—plants reabsorb CO2 as they grow—but life-cycle analyses (David Pimentel, 2005) show many biofuels yield only marginal net energy gains. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify biomass as renewable but not always carbon-neutral.

questions.prompt: Long-term shift in temperature and weather patterns globally.
questions.explanation: Climate change is long-term changes in Earth's temperature and weather patterns, increasingly driven by human greenhouse gas emissions. The IPCC (founded 1988) is the leading international scientific body. Earth's average surface temperature has risen ~1.1 degrees Celsius since 1880 per NASA GISTEMP, with the warmest 10 years on record all occurring since 2010. The Keeling Curve shows atmospheric CO2 rising from 315 ppm (1958) to over 420 ppm (2023). Consequences: rising seas (~3.4 mm/year currently), melting glaciers (Arctic sea ice down 13%/decade), more extreme weather, ocean acidification. NYS Grade 7 Science exams link fossil fuel CO2 to global warming.

questions.prompt: Pollution of land, water, or air by harmful materials from human activity.
questions.explanation: Pollution is the introduction of harmful substances into the environment, affecting air, water, soil, or living things. Rachel Carson's 1962 Silent Spring exposed DDT pesticide pollution, launching the modern environmental movement and contributing to the 1970 founding of the US EPA and the Clean Air Act. Major types: air pollution (smog, particulates, NOx, SOx, ozone), water pollution (oil spills, runoff, plastic), soil pollution (pesticides, heavy metals). The 2010 BP Deepwater Horizon oil spill released ~4.9 million barrels. Indoor air pollution kills ~3.2 million people/year (WHO). NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify pollution as a major human impact.

questions.prompt: Permanent removal of forest, reducing biodiversity and increasing CO2.
questions.explanation: Deforestation is permanent forest loss for agriculture, logging, or development, removing carbon sinks and habitat. The Amazon rainforest has lost ~17% of its area since 1970, primarily for cattle ranching and soybean farming; Brazil's deforestation tripled under 2019-22 policies. Tropical deforestation contributes ~10% of global CO2 emissions and causes ~50% of biodiversity loss. The 1979 work of ecologists like Norman Myers documented hotspot forest loss. Indonesia and Malaysia have cleared ~13 million hectares of rainforest for palm oil plantations since 2000. NYS Grade 7 Science exams link deforestation to climate change and biodiversity loss.

questions.prompt: Loss of species and habitats due to human pressures.
questions.explanation: Biodiversity loss is the rapid decline of species and ecosystems, often called the Sixth Mass Extinction. The 2019 IPBES report warned that ~1 million species face extinction within decades—the highest rate in human history, ~100-1,000 times pre-human baseline. The 2022 WWF Living Planet Report documented a 69% average decline in monitored wildlife populations since 1970. Major drivers (Bradshaw et al., 2021): habitat destruction, climate change, pollution, invasive species, overharvesting. Examples: passenger pigeon (extinct 1914), thylacine (extinct 1936), Yangtze River dolphin (extinct 2007), monarch butterflies (-90% since 1996). NYS Grade 7 Science exams require linking biodiversity loss to human pressures.

questions.prompt: Using resources at a rate that meets present needs without harming the future.
questions.explanation: Sustainability is the use of resources to meet current needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs. The 1987 Brundtland Commission report Our Common Future, chaired by Norwegian PM Gro Harlem Brundtland, established this definition and the concept of sustainable development. The 2015 UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) set 17 targets including climate action, clean water, and life below water. Sustainability requires balancing economic, social, and environmental needs—the triple bottom line proposed by John Elkington (1994). Examples: sustainable fisheries (Marine Stewardship Council, 1997), FSC-certified forests, renewable energy. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test sustainability as a guiding environmental principle.

questions.prompt: Process of reusing materials like aluminum, paper, and glass.
questions.explanation: Recycling reprocesses used materials into new products, conserving raw resources and energy. Aluminum recycling saves 95% of the energy needed for primary production from bauxite (a 1972 EPA analysis). The first US curbside recycling program began in Berkeley, California, in 1973. Today, the global recycling rate is ~9% for plastics (Roland Geyer et al., 2017 Science Advances), ~70% for steel, ~75% for aluminum cans. The Mobius loop recycling symbol was designed by Gary Anderson in 1970 for a contest by the Container Corporation of America. Single-stream recycling (Steve Skadron, 1992) increased participation. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify recycling among the 3 R's.

questions.prompt: Protecting an area's natural ecosystem and species from development.
questions.explanation: Conservation is the management and protection of natural resources, ecosystems, and species. The American conservation movement was launched by John Muir (founded Sierra Club 1892) and Theodore Roosevelt (signed the 1906 Antiquities Act, established 5 national parks, 18 national monuments, and 51 wildlife refuges). The US National Park System (1916) and Wilderness Act (1964) protected ~110 million acres. Globally, ~17% of land and ~8% of marine areas are protected (CBD Aichi targets). Examples: Yellowstone (1872, world's first national park), the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park (1975), Yosemite (1890). NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast conservation (protection) with preservation (no human use).

questions.prompt: Group of species at risk of going extinct in the near future.
questions.explanation: An endangered species faces imminent extinction risk. The 1973 US Endangered Species Act (signed by Nixon) protected the bald eagle, gray wolf, and grizzly bear. The IUCN Red List (founded 1964) classifies species from Least Concern to Extinct in the Wild. As of 2023, ~28% of assessed species (~42,100) are threatened. Recovery successes include the bald eagle (delisted 2007 after DDT ban), peregrine falcon (delisted 1999), and American alligator (delisted 1987). Ongoing crises: the Vaquita porpoise (<20 left), Sumatran orangutan (~14,600 left), and northern white rhino (2 remaining females). NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the IUCN classifications and ESA protections.

questions.prompt: Resource that can be replaced naturally within a human lifetime.
questions.explanation: A renewable resource is one that nature replenishes within a human timeframe, in contrast to fossil fuels formed over millions of years. Examples include solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, biomass, and freshwater (rapidly renewable if managed). The Renewable Portfolio Standards adopted by 30 US states since 1983 (Iowa was first) require utilities to source a percentage from renewables. Globally, renewables provided 29% of electricity in 2022 (IEA), up from ~18% in 2000. Hydroelectric is the largest (16%), then wind (~7%), solar (~5%). NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast renewable vs nonrenewable and identify solar, wind, hydro as renewable.

questions.prompt: Protected land set aside for wildlife and natural resources.
questions.explanation: A wildlife refuge is land managed primarily for the conservation of fish, wildlife, and habitat. President Theodore Roosevelt established the first US National Wildlife Refuge—Pelican Island, Florida—on March 14, 1903, to protect brown pelicans from plume hunters. The US National Wildlife Refuge System now contains 567 refuges covering 95 million acres, managed by the US Fish and Wildlife Service since 1940. The Arctic National Wildlife Refuge (1960, 19 million acres) protects polar bears, caribou, and migratory birds. New York's Montezuma NWR on the Cayuga-Seneca canal protects 50,000 waterfowl yearly. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test wildlife refuges as protected conservation areas.

questions.prompt: Smallest unit of an element that retains its chemical properties.
questions.explanation: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains the element's chemical properties. The concept dates to Greek philosopher Democritus (~450 BCE), who coined atomos meaning indivisible. John Dalton's 1808 atomic theory (A New System of Chemical Philosophy) established atoms as the foundation of modern chemistry: atoms are indivisible, atoms of an element are identical, and compounds form from atom combinations in fixed ratios. Modern atoms are now known to be divisible into protons, neutrons, and electrons. The smallest atom is hydrogen (~0.1 nm diameter); a teaspoon of solid material contains ~10^23 atoms. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require atoms as elementary chemical units.

questions.prompt: Positively charged particle in an atom's nucleus.
questions.explanation: A proton is a positively charged subatomic particle in the nucleus, with mass ~1.673e-27 kg (1 atomic mass unit). Ernest Rutherford discovered the proton in 1917-1919 by bombarding nitrogen with alpha particles. The number of protons defines the element—hydrogen has 1, carbon 6, oxygen 8, uranium 92. This number is the atomic number, key to the periodic table arrangement by Dmitri Mendeleev (1869) and Henry Moseley (1913). Protons in the nucleus are bound by the strong nuclear force, overcoming their mutual electrostatic repulsion. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify the atomic number as proton count.

questions.prompt: Neutral nuclear particle with mass equal to a proton's.
questions.explanation: A neutron is a neutral subatomic particle in the nucleus with mass slightly greater than a proton (~1.675e-27 kg). James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932 at Cambridge's Cavendish Laboratory, winning the 1935 Nobel Prize. Neutrons add to atomic mass without changing chemical identity. Isotopes—atoms with the same proton count but different neutron count—include carbon-12 (6 neutrons), carbon-13 (7), carbon-14 (8, used in radiocarbon dating). Neutron-rich isotopes are often radioactive. Free neutrons decay with a half-life of ~10.6 minutes. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to calculate neutron count: mass number minus atomic number.

questions.prompt: Negatively charged particle that orbits an atom's nucleus.
questions.explanation: An electron is a negatively charged subatomic particle that occupies regions around the nucleus called orbitals, with mass only 1/1836 that of a proton. J.J. Thomson discovered the electron in 1897 at Cambridge using cathode ray tubes, winning the 1906 Nobel Prize. Niels Bohr proposed quantized electron orbits in 1913. Electrons in the outermost shell (valence electrons) determine chemical bonding behavior. Erwin Schrodinger's 1926 wave equation describes the modern probabilistic orbital model. A neutral atom has equal protons and electrons; gaining or losing electrons forms ions. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify electrons as negative particles that determine bonding.

questions.prompt: Dense central region of an atom containing protons and neutrons.
questions.explanation: The atomic nucleus is the dense central region containing protons and neutrons (collectively nucleons), holding ~99.95% of an atom's mass in only ~1/100,000 of its volume. Ernest Rutherford discovered the nucleus in 1911 through his famous gold foil experiment with Hans Geiger and Ernest Marsden, in which alpha particles deflected back from a thin gold foil—revealing the atom's dense, positive core. The nucleus diameter is ~10^-14 m, while the whole atom is ~10^-10 m—if a nucleus were a pea, the atom would be a football stadium. Strong nuclear force holds nucleons together. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require nucleus as the proton/neutron container.

questions.prompt: Russian chemist who arranged elements by properties in 1869.
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev (1834-1907), a Russian chemist at St. Petersburg University, published the first widely accepted periodic table in 1869. He arranged the 63 known elements by atomic mass into rows where columns shared properties. Remarkably, he left blank spaces for undiscovered elements and predicted their properties—eka-aluminum (gallium, discovered 1875), eka-boron (scandium, 1879), and eka-silicon (germanium, 1886) matched his predictions, vindicating his system. The periodic law was later reformulated by Henry Moseley in 1913 to use atomic number (proton count) rather than mass. Element 101 was named mendelevium in 1955. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Mendeleev as the periodic table creator.

questions.prompt: Substance made of only one type of atom, listed on the periodic table.
questions.explanation: An element is a pure substance consisting of only one type of atom (one atomic number) and cannot be broken into simpler substances by chemical means. Robert Boyle defined elements operationally in The Sceptical Chymist (1661). The periodic table contains 118 known elements (as of 2024). Naturally occurring elements: 94 (1-94, though some are radioactive trace amounts); synthesized: 95-118. Hydrogen is the most abundant element in the universe (~75% by mass); oxygen, silicon, and aluminum dominate Earth's crust.
