Element 117 (tennessine) was synthesized in 2010 at Oak Ridge National Laboratory. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test element vs compound vs mixture distinctions.

questions.prompt: Vertical column of elements with similar chemical properties.
questions.explanation: A group is a vertical column of the periodic table containing elements with the same number of valence electrons and therefore similar chemical properties. The periodic table has 18 groups. Group 1 (alkali metals: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) are soft, react violently with water; Group 17 (halogens: F, Cl, Br, I, At) form salts; Group 18 (noble gases: He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) are largely unreactive. Mendeleev arranged elements in groups by similar properties in 1869, though he then had only 8 groups. Henry Moseley confirmed the structure with atomic numbers in 1913. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test group properties, especially alkali metals and halogens.

questions.prompt: Horizontal row on the periodic table, also called a period.
questions.explanation: A period is a horizontal row in the periodic table, corresponding to the principal quantum number of valence electrons (the energy level being filled). The modern table has 7 periods. Period 1 contains only H and He (filling the 1s shell); Period 2 (Li to Ne) fills the 2s and 2p shells; Period 7 includes the recently confirmed superheavy elements 113-118 (nihonium, moscovium, tennessine, oganesson) approved by IUPAC in 2016. Moving left-to-right within a period, atomic radius decreases, ionization energy increases, and electronegativity increases (with the noble gases as exceptions). NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the trend within periods.

questions.prompt: Outermost electrons that determine an atom's bonding behavior.
questions.explanation: Valence electrons are the outermost shell electrons that participate in chemical bonding. Gilbert N. Lewis introduced the concept and the Lewis dot structures in 1916. Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a stable 8-electron outer shell (the octet rule), which Linus Pauling formalized in 1939. For main-group elements, the group number indicates valence electrons: Group 1 has 1, Group 2 has 2, Group 13 has 3, ... Group 18 has 8 (except helium with 2). Sodium (1 valence electron) easily loses it to form Na+; chlorine (7 valence electrons) gains 1 to form Cl-, forming NaCl. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require valence electron count for bonding predictions.

questions.prompt: Process in which substances combine or break apart to form new substances.
questions.explanation: A chemical reaction is a process that transforms reactants into products by breaking and forming chemical bonds, creating substances with new properties. Antoine Lavoisier established quantitative chemistry in his 1789 Traite Elementaire de Chimie, identifying that mass is conserved in reactions. John Dalton's 1808 atomic theory explained reactions as atom rearrangements. Reaction evidence includes color change, temperature change, gas production (bubbles), precipitate formation, light or sound emission. Reactions are written as equations with reactants on the left and products on the right (e.g., 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O). NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast chemical reactions (new substance) with physical changes.

questions.prompt: Starting substances in a chemical reaction, written on the left of an arrow.
questions.explanation: Reactants are the starting substances consumed in a chemical reaction, conventionally written on the left side of a chemical equation. In the equation 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O, hydrogen and oxygen are reactants. The arrow's direction indicates the reaction proceeds left-to-right; reversible reactions use a double arrow. Antoine Lavoisier's 1789 Law of Conservation of Mass requires the same atoms appear in reactants and products—only their arrangement changes. Limiting reactants (described by Theodore Richards, 1900s) are the reactant consumed first, capping product yield. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test identification of reactants vs products in equations.

questions.prompt: Substances produced by a chemical reaction, written on the right of an arrow.
questions.explanation: Products are the new substances formed by a chemical reaction, conventionally written on the right side of a chemical equation. In photosynthesis (6CO2 + 6H2O -> C6H12O6 + 6O2), glucose and oxygen are products. Antoine Lavoisier's 1772 combustion experiments showed that mass of products equals mass of reactants, leading to the Law of Conservation of Mass. Products have different properties than reactants—iron rust (Fe2O3) is reddish-brown despite iron being silver-gray. The 1968 Nobel-winning Belousov-Zhabotinsky oscillating reactions periodically reverse products and reactants. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify reactants vs products.

questions.prompt: Reaction releasing heat, like combustion of wood or gas.
questions.explanation: An exothermic reaction releases energy (usually heat) to the surroundings; products have less chemical potential energy than reactants. The term was coined by Marcellin Berthelot in 1875. Examples include combustion (methane burning releases 891 kJ/mol), neutralization (acid + base), respiration in cells, and most explosions. Igniting magnesium in oxygen produces magnesium oxide and 1,204 kJ/mol of heat plus blinding white light. Hand warmers using iron oxidation are practical exothermic reactions. Exothermic reactions feel warm; the energy diagram shows products at lower energy than reactants. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast exothermic (releases heat) with endothermic (absorbs heat).

questions.prompt: Reaction absorbing heat from surroundings, like an instant cold pack.
questions.explanation: An endothermic reaction absorbs energy from the surroundings; products have more chemical potential energy than reactants. Marcellin Berthelot coined the term in 1875. Examples: photosynthesis (absorbing solar energy), melting ice, dissolving ammonium nitrate in water (used in instant cold packs, dropping temperature 30 degrees Celsius below ambient), and most cooking processes. Endothermic reactions feel cold because they pull heat from the environment. The Haber-Bosch process for ammonia is exothermic forward but the reverse decomposition is endothermic. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test endothermic identification by cooling (cold pack) vs exothermic by warming.

questions.prompt: Law stating mass is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: The Law of Conservation of Mass states that mass is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions—only rearranged. Antoine Lavoisier established this experimentally in 1772-1789 by weighing reactants and products in sealed vessels, founding modern chemistry. His famous 1772 experiment burned tin in a sealed flask; the total mass was unchanged, though the air mass decreased and the tin gained the missing oxygen mass. Mikhail Lomonosov anticipated this in 1748 in Russia. The principle was extended to mass-energy equivalence by Einstein's E=mc^2 in 1905, where nuclear reactions convert tiny amounts of mass to energy. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require balanced equations demonstrating conservation.

questions.prompt: 1789 French chemist who founded modern chemistry by quantifying reactions.
questions.explanation: Antoine Lavoisier (1743-1794) is the father of modern chemistry. His 1789 textbook Traite Elementaire de Chimie established conservation of mass, named oxygen (1777) and hydrogen (1783), identified water as H2O, and overthrew the phlogiston theory of combustion. With his wife Marie-Anne Paulze Lavoisier (his lab partner and illustrator), he proved that combustion is rapid combination with oxygen. He also developed the first modern list of 33 elements. As a French nobleman and tax collector during the Revolution, he was guillotined on May 8, 1794; mathematician Joseph-Louis Lagrange remarked it took only a moment to cut off that head but France will not produce another in a century. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Lavoisier with conservation of mass.

questions.prompt: Equation showing reactants becoming products with conserved atoms.
questions.explanation: A balanced chemical equation has equal numbers of each atom on the reactant and product sides, satisfying the Law of Conservation of Mass. John Dalton's 1808 atomic theory made balancing logical. Coefficients (not subscripts) are adjusted to balance: H2 + O2 -> H2O is unbalanced; 2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O is balanced (4 H, 2 O each side). The classic combustion equation CH4 + 2O2 -> CO2 + 2H2O has 1 C, 4 H, 4 O on each side. Stoichiometric coefficients enable scaling—the Haber-Bosch ammonia synthesis (N2 + 3H2 -> 2NH3) produces ~150 million tons/year industrially. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to balance simple equations.

questions.prompt: Smallest neutral particle of a compound made of two or more atoms.
questions.explanation: A molecule is the smallest neutral particle of a compound or pure substance composed of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds. Italian chemist Amedeo Avogadro proposed in 1811 that equal volumes of gases contain equal numbers of molecules (Avogadro's Law, accepted ~1860 after Stanislao Cannizzaro). Diatomic molecules (H2, O2, N2, Cl2) contain two atoms. Water (H2O) has 2 hydrogen + 1 oxygen. Atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) has 1 carbon + 2 oxygen. The largest known biological molecule is human chromosome 1's DNA with ~250 million base pairs. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to read molecular formulas like H2O, CO2, NaCl.

questions.prompt: Substance made of two or more elements chemically bonded together.
questions.explanation: A compound is a pure substance formed from two or more different elements chemically bonded in fixed ratios. Joseph Louis Proust formulated the Law of Definite Proportions in 1799: pure water is always 11.2% hydrogen and 88.8% oxygen by mass, regardless of source. Compounds have properties different from their constituent elements—sodium (explosive metal) + chlorine (poison gas) -> sodium chloride (edible table salt). The 2023 PubChem database catalogs 113 million chemical compounds. Compounds can be broken down chemically (not physically) into elements. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast compounds (chemically bonded, fixed ratio) with mixtures (physically combined, variable ratio).

questions.prompt: Change in shape or state without forming new substances, like ice melting.
questions.explanation: A physical change alters the form or state of matter without changing chemical identity—no new substance forms. Examples: melting, freezing, boiling, dissolving, crushing, cutting, magnetizing. Ice (H2O solid) melts to water (H2O liquid) but remains H2O. Hermann von Helmholtz formalized phase change physics in the 19th century. Physical changes can usually be reversed (refreezing water). Dissolving salt in water is physical—the salt can be recovered by evaporation. Sublimation (dry ice CO2 going directly from solid to gas) is also physical. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to distinguish physical from chemical changes by absence of new substances.

questions.prompt: Three common phases of matter—solid, liquid, and this gaseous form.
questions.explanation: Gas is one of the four fundamental states of matter, characterized by particles in random rapid motion that fill any container, with no fixed shape or volume. Robert Boyle's 1662 Boyle's Law (PV = constant at constant T) was the first quantitative gas law; Jacques Charles's 1787 law (V/T constant) and Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac's 1809 law (P/T constant) completed the trio. The Ideal Gas Law PV=nRT, formalized by Émile Clapeyron in 1834, unified them. Air at standard conditions has ~10^25 molecules per cubic meter. Plasma—ionized gas—is sometimes called the fourth state. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the three common states (solid, liquid, gas) and the property changes.

questions.prompt: Phase change from liquid to gas at the boiling point.
questions.explanation: Boiling is the phase change from liquid to gas when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure throughout the liquid, producing bubbles. Pure water boils at 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit) at sea-level atmospheric pressure (1 atm); on Mt. Everest's summit (~0.33 atm), water boils at ~70 degrees Celsius—why high-altitude cooking takes longer. Hermann von Helmholtz and Lord Kelvin formalized thermodynamics including boiling in the 1840s-50s. The heat of vaporization for water (~2,260 J/g) is unusually high due to hydrogen bonding, making water a superb coolant. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test boiling as physical change.

questions.prompt: Property like color, hardness, or density that does not change a substance's identity.
questions.explanation: Physical properties are characteristics observable without changing chemical identity: color, density, mass, volume, melting point, boiling point, hardness, conductivity, malleability, ductility. Friedrich Mohs's 1812 hardness scale and Hans Christian Oersted's 1820 conductivity work established systematic physical property measurement. Water's physical properties include density (1.00 g/mL at 4 degrees Celsius), melting point (0 degrees Celsius), boiling point (100 degrees Celsius), and surface tension (~72 mN/m). NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast physical properties (observable without reaction) with chemical properties (revealed by reactions like flammability).

questions.prompt: Property describing how a substance reacts, such as flammability or rusting.
questions.explanation: Chemical properties describe how substances react to form new substances—revealed only through chemical change. Examples: flammability (wood burns), reactivity with acids (zinc reacts with HCl producing H2 gas), reactivity with water (sodium reacts violently; gold does not), oxidation (iron rusts in moist air to Fe2O3). The activity series of metals, developed by William Odling and refined throughout the 1800s, ranks metals by reactivity (most: potassium, sodium; least: gold, platinum). Antoine Lavoisier's 1789 work on combustion established reactivity as a measurable chemical property. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast chemical with physical properties using flammability and reactivity.

questions.prompt: English scientist who described the three laws of motion in 1687.
questions.explanation: Sir Isaac Newton (1642-1727) published Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica in 1687, presenting his three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation. Working at Trinity College, Cambridge, Newton also invented calculus (independently of Leibniz), described the spectrum of white light (1666), and formulated the binomial theorem. His three laws of motion explain everything from a falling apple to planetary orbits. Newton was knighted by Queen Anne in 1705 and served as Master of the Royal Mint. His Principia laid the foundation for classical mechanics until Einstein's 1905 special relativity and 1915 general relativity modified its application at extreme speeds and gravities. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Newton with the three laws.

questions.prompt: Newton's first law: an object at rest stays at rest unless acted on by a force.
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) states that an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion continues at constant velocity unless acted on by a net external force. Galileo Galilei first proposed inertia in 1632, contradicting Aristotle's view that motion required continuous force. Newton formalized it in 1687. Inertia is proportional to mass—a bowling ball has more inertia than a tennis ball. Seatbelts protect passengers because in a crash, the car decelerates but the passenger's inertia keeps them moving forward. The 1958 Volvo three-point seatbelt (Nils Bohlin) has saved an estimated million lives. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test inertia with stationary objects.

questions.prompt: Newton's second law equation relating force, mass, and acceleration.
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law states F = ma: net force on an object equals mass times acceleration. The law appears in Principia Mathematica (1687). Force is measured in newtons (N); 1 N = 1 kg*m/s^2. A 1 kg mass accelerated 1 m/s^2 requires 1 N of force. A 1,500 kg car braking at 5 m/s^2 needs 7,500 N of braking force. Larger mass requires more force for the same acceleration—a truck takes longer to brake than a motorcycle. Acceleration is directly proportional to force and inversely proportional to mass. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test F=ma calculations and the unit newton.

questions.prompt: Newton's third law: every action force has an equal and opposite force.
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction—forces always occur in pairs acting on different objects. Newton formulated this in 1687. A rocket lifting off exemplifies this: the rocket pushes exhaust gases downward (action), and the gases push the rocket upward (reaction)—the same principle behind Robert Goddard's first liquid-fueled rocket (1926) and the Saturn V (1967-73) lifting astronauts to the Moon. Walking depends on the third law: feet push back on the ground, ground pushes forward on feet. A swimmer pushes water back, water pushes swimmer forward. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test action-reaction with rockets, jumping, swimming.

questions.prompt: Force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact.
questions.explanation: Friction is the force that resists relative motion between surfaces in contact. Charles-Augustin de Coulomb developed laws of friction in 1781, identifying static (preventing motion start), kinetic (resisting moving object), and rolling (slowing rolling objects). Friction depends on the normal force and the coefficient of friction (mu), which varies by surface pair—rubber on dry asphalt mu~0.9, ice on ice mu~0.01. Without friction, walking and driving would be impossible. Lubricants (oil, ball bearings) reduce friction, increasing machinery efficiency—Egyptian pyramid builders may have used water-lubricated wooden sledges (Daniel Bonn's 2014 Physical Review Letters study). NYS Grade 7 Science exams test friction as resistive force.

questions.prompt: Force that pulls objects toward Earth and toward each other.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the attractive force between any two objects with mass; it shapes the universe at large scales. Isaac Newton's 1687 law of universal gravitation states F = G*m1*m2/r^2, where G = 6.674e-11 N*m^2/kg^2 (measured by Henry Cavendish in 1798 with his torsion balance). Earth's surface gravitational acceleration g = 9.8 m/s^2 means a 1 kg object weighs 9.8 N. Einstein's 1915 General Relativity reinterpreted gravity as curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy. Gravity holds the Moon in orbit around Earth, Earth around the Sun, and binds galaxies. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify gravity as the force pulling toward Earth.

questions.prompt: Acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface in meters per second squared.
questions.explanation: The acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface is approximately 9.8 m/s^2 (more precisely 9.80665 m/s^2 at sea level, mid-latitudes). Galileo Galilei measured falling-body acceleration in his 1604 Pisa experiments, finding all objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass (in vacuum). Newton's 1687 universal gravitation explained why: F = mg, and a = F/m = g, so mass cancels. The value varies with latitude (centrifugal effect: ~9.78 at equator, ~9.83 at poles) and altitude. Apollo 15 astronaut David Scott dramatically demonstrated this on the Moon (1971) by dropping a hammer and feather together. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require ~9.8 m/s^2 as g.

questions.prompt: Pull of Earth's gravity on an object, measured in newtons.
questions.explanation: Weight is the gravitational force exerted on an object by a planet: W = mg, where m is mass (kg) and g is gravitational acceleration (m/s^2). On Earth, weight in newtons equals mass in kilograms times 9.8. A 60 kg person weighs 588 N on Earth. Weight differs by location: the same person weighs ~99 N on the Moon (g = 1.62 m/s^2) and 0 N in deep space. Mass remains constant; weight does not. The kilogram is the SI unit of mass, redefined in 2019 via Planck's constant; the newton is the SI unit of force, named for Newton. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast weight (force, varies) with mass (matter quantity, constant).

questions.prompt: Amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms.
questions.explanation: Mass is the measure of an object's matter content and its resistance to acceleration (inertia). The SI unit is the kilogram, redefined in May 2019 using Planck's constant h (= 6.62607015e-34 J*s) by the International Bureau of Weights and Measures. Mass is constant regardless of location; weight changes with gravity. The Higgs boson, discovered at CERN in 2012, explains how fundamental particles acquire mass via the Higgs mechanism (Peter Higgs and Francois Englert proposed it in 1964; they shared the 2013 Nobel Prize). Einstein's 1905 E=mc^2 showed mass-energy equivalence. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the kilogram as the SI unit of mass, separate from weight.

questions.prompt: Sensation of weightlessness experienced when falling freely in a gravitational field.
questions.explanation: Free fall is motion under gravity alone, with no other forces (no air resistance, no support). In free fall, all objects accelerate at g (9.8 m/s^2 on Earth, regardless of mass)—as Galileo first proposed in 1604 and Apollo 15's David Scott demonstrated on the Moon in 1971 by dropping a hammer and feather together. Astronauts in the International Space Station orbiting at ~408 km altitude are in continuous free fall, giving the appearance of weightlessness (microgravity). The ISS travels 7.66 km/s, falling around Earth instead of into it. Skydivers reach terminal velocity (~53 m/s) when air resistance balances gravity. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test free fall as gravitational acceleration.

questions.prompt: Push or pull that can change an object's motion.
questions.explanation: A force is a push or pull that can change the motion (velocity), shape, or state of an object. Forces have magnitude AND direction (vectors). Isaac Newton's 1687 second law defines force as F = ma. The SI unit is the newton (N), where 1 N = 1 kg*m/s^2. Common forces include gravity, friction, normal force, tension, applied force, air resistance, magnetism, and electric force. Free-body diagrams (introduced by James Clerk Maxwell in the 1860s) show all forces on an object as arrows. When net force is zero, the object is in equilibrium. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify the newton as the SI unit of force.

questions.prompt: Total force resulting when multiple forces act on one object.
questions.explanation: Net force is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object. When forces act in the same direction, they add; in opposite directions, they subtract. Isaac Newton's second law uses net force: F_net = ma. If a 100 N person pushes a box forward and 30 N of friction opposes, net force = 70 N forward. Net force zero means the object is in equilibrium (at rest or constant velocity). Two students in a tug-of-war pulling 80 N each produce zero net force. The 1851 Foucault pendulum demonstrated Earth's rotation by tracking how net forces shifted apparent motion. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test net force = sum of all forces accounting for direction.

questions.prompt: Equal forces acting in opposite directions, producing no motion change.
questions.explanation: Balanced forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, producing zero net force and no change in motion (Newton's First Law). A book resting on a table has gravity (downward) balanced by normal force (upward). Skydivers reaching terminal velocity have gravity balanced by air resistance, falling at constant speed (~53 m/s). The Hoover Dam (built 1931-36) holds back Lake Mead's 35 million acre-feet by balancing water pressure with the dam's massive structure. Statics—the study of bodies in balanced forces—dates to Archimedes (~250 BCE) and underlies all civil engineering. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast balanced (no motion change) with unbalanced (acceleration).

questions.prompt: Forces that change an object's motion, producing acceleration.
questions.explanation: Unbalanced forces have a nonzero net force and cause acceleration—change in velocity (speed or direction). Isaac Newton's Second Law: F = ma. When a soccer player kicks a ball, the kick force is unbalanced (other forces on the ball are zero or minimal in flight), causing acceleration. A 1,500 kg car experiencing 6,000 N net forward force accelerates at 4 m/s^2 (F/m). The 2009 Tohoku earthquake (magnitude 9.1) demonstrated unbalanced tectonic forces shifting Japan ~2.4 meters east. Roller coasters use unbalanced forces (gravity changing direction) to thrill riders. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test unbalanced forces as producers of acceleration.

questions.prompt: Resistance to a moving object's motion through air or fluid.
questions.explanation: Air resistance (drag) is the friction-like force opposing motion through air or any fluid; it increases with speed and the object's frontal area. The drag equation F_d = 0.5*rho*v^2*Cd*A was developed by Lord Rayleigh in 1876. A skydiver belly-down has more drag (terminal velocity ~53 m/s) than head-down (~90 m/s); a falling feather has so much drag relative to weight that it drifts slowly. In a vacuum, all objects fall at g together—Apollo 15's David Scott demonstrated this with hammer and feather on the Moon in 1971. Streamlined shapes (Boeing 787, Tesla Model S) minimize drag, improving fuel efficiency. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test air resistance as a force opposing motion.

questions.prompt: Simple machine consisting of a rigid bar pivoting on a fixed point.
questions.explanation: A lever is a rigid bar that pivots around a fulcrum to multiply force or distance. Greek mathematician Archimedes formalized the principle in the 3rd century BCE with the equation Force1*d1 = Force2*d2; he reportedly said Give me a place to stand and I shall move the Earth. Three classes exist based on fulcrum position: Class 1 (seesaw, scissors), Class 2 (wheelbarrow), Class 3 (forearm, tweezers). The mechanical advantage is the ratio of load arm to effort arm. A 2-meter crowbar with fulcrum 20 cm from load can lift a 500 kg stone with only 56 kg of force. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify levers as force multipliers.

questions.prompt: Inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder, used to fasten objects.
questions.explanation: A screw is a simple machine consisting of an inclined plane wrapped around a cylindrical shaft. Archimedes designed the Archimedean screw around 250 BCE for irrigating fields and bilge pumping. Pitch (distance between threads) determines mechanical advantage—finer threads (more revolutions to advance) provide greater force multiplication. Modern screw threads were standardized by Joseph Whitworth in 1841 (British Standard Whitworth) and Henry Maudslay's 1800 screw-cutting lathe enabled mass production. Examples: wood screws, jar lids, drill bits, vises, propellers. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify screws as inclined-plane variants and force multipliers.

questions.prompt: Sloped surface used to raise loads with less force, like a ramp.
questions.explanation: An inclined plane is a flat surface tilted at an angle to reduce the force needed to raise a load, at the cost of moving it a longer distance. The work principle (W = Fd) means trading higher force for shorter distance becomes lower force for longer distance. Egyptian pyramid builders may have used inclined planes ~2,500 BCE to raise stone blocks (Mark Lehner's 2015 archaeological reconstruction). Wheelchair ramps required by the 1990 US Americans with Disabilities Act use shallow inclines (1:12 maximum slope) for accessibility. Mechanical advantage = length / height. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify ramps as force-reducing simple machines.

questions.prompt: Grooved wheel with a rope used to change the direction of force.
questions.explanation: A pulley is a grooved wheel with a rope or cable that changes the direction of force or, in compound systems (block and tackle), multiplies force. Archimedes used compound pulleys in the 3rd century BCE to single-handedly launch a fully loaded ship for King Hiero II. A single fixed pulley has mechanical advantage 1 (just changes direction); two pulleys halve the force needed (MA=2). Elevators (Elisha Otis's 1854 safety brake) and cranes rely on pulleys. The 280-meter-tall Burj Khalifa elevator system (2010, world's tallest building) uses sophisticated pulley/cable systems. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify pulleys as direction-changers and force-multipliers.

questions.prompt: Mechanical advantage of a simple machine measured as output force divided by input force.
questions.explanation: Mechanical Advantage (MA) is the ratio of output force to input force in a simple machine, measuring how much it multiplies effort: MA = F_out / F_in. Archimedes derived the principle in the 3rd century BCE. Real machines have MA less than ideal due to friction losses (efficiency < 100%). Examples: a 1-meter inclined plane raising a load 0.25 m has MA = 4 (only 1/4 the lifting force needed); a 1:4 block-and-tackle has MA = 4. The work principle (W = Fd) means high-MA machines trade greater distance for less force—conservation of energy is preserved. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test MA = output force / input force calculations.

questions.prompt: Distance traveled per unit of time, like miles per hour.
questions.explanation: Speed is the rate of distance change with time: v = d/t. Galileo Galilei first measured speeds quantitatively in his 1604 inclined plane experiments. Common units: m/s (SI), km/h, mph (1 mph = 0.447 m/s). Speed is a scalar (no direction); velocity is the vector form (speed + direction). Average speed is total distance over total time; instantaneous speed is at one moment. The speed of light c = 299,792,458 m/s is nature's universal speed limit (Einstein, 1905). Usain Bolt's 9.58-second 100m world record (Berlin 2009) averaged ~10.4 m/s. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test speed = distance / time calculations.

questions.prompt: Rate of change of speed, occurring whenever an object speeds up or slows.
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity (speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction): a = (vf - vi)/t. SI unit: m/s^2. Galileo Galilei measured acceleration of falling objects in 1604; Newton incorporated it in F=ma in 1687. Earth's gravitational acceleration at the surface is 9.8 m/s^2. A Tesla Model S Plaid reaches 0 to 60 mph in 1.99 seconds, acceleration ~13.5 m/s^2. Negative acceleration (deceleration) means slowing down. Centripetal acceleration (Christiaan Huygens, 1659) keeps moving objects on curved paths. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test acceleration as change-in-velocity over time.

questions.prompt: Speed in a specific direction, a vector quantity.
questions.explanation: Velocity is speed with direction—a vector quantity. Isaac Newton's 1687 Principia distinguishes velocity from speed. A car traveling 60 mph north has the same speed as one going 60 mph south but different velocity. Changes in velocity (acceleration) include speeding up, slowing down, OR changing direction—a car rounding a curve at constant speed is accelerating (direction changes). Vectors are represented by arrows; sums use the parallelogram rule (Pierre Varignon, 1687) or component method (Rene Descartes, 1637). The Doppler effect (Christian Doppler, 1842) shifts wave frequency based on relative velocity. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast scalar speed with vector velocity.

questions.prompt: Graph showing distance on the y-axis versus time on the x-axis.
questions.explanation: A distance-time graph plots distance (y-axis) versus time (x-axis); the slope = distance/time = speed. A horizontal line means the object is stationary (no distance change); a straight slanted line means constant speed; a curve means acceleration. Galileo Galilei's 1604 inclined plane experiments showed that distance is proportional to t^2 for uniform acceleration—on graph paper, a parabola. Steeper slope = faster speed. Reading slope = rise/run gives speed in same units (m/s). NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to read distance-time graphs and identify the steeper slope as the faster object, horizontal as stationary.

questions.prompt: Object's position relative to a reference point, also called this in physics.
questions.explanation: Displacement is the vector from an object's starting position to its ending position—a straight-line distance with direction, not the actual path traveled. Isaac Newton's 1687 work used displacement implicitly; the formal vector notation was developed by William Rowan Hamilton in 1843 with quaternions and later by Josiah Willard Gibbs (1881-84). A runner who completes a 400m track lap has 400m distance traveled but zero displacement (same start/end point). Displacement determines velocity (displacement/time) and is the integral of velocity over time. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast displacement (vector, straight line) with distance (scalar, total path).

questions.prompt: Process in meiosis where homologous chromosomes exchange DNA segments.
questions.explanation: Crossing over (chiasma formation) occurs in prophase I of meiosis when homologous chromosomes exchange corresponding DNA segments, shuffling maternal and paternal alleles. Thomas Hunt Morgan first proposed it in 1911 using Drosophila melanogaster fruit flies; his student Alfred Sturtevant used recombination frequencies to build the first genetic map in 1913. Crossing over—combined with independent assortment and random fertilization—creates genetic variation in offspring. Without it, siblings would inherit only parental allele combinations. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify crossing over as a key source of genetic variation alongside the other meiotic shuffles.

questions.prompt: Organism that breaks down dead matter, returning nutrients to soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers—primarily bacteria and fungi—break down dead organic matter, releasing nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and carbon back into the soil for producers. Anton de Bary established the saprophytic role of fungi in the 1860s. Decomposers process ~99% of organic matter on Earth: a single gram of forest soil contains over a billion bacteria and ~50,000 fungal hyphae. Without decomposers, dead matter would accumulate and nutrients would be locked away, halting the carbon and nitrogen cycles. Earthworms (Charles Darwin's last book, 1881) help break down leaf litter. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify decomposers as ecosystem recyclers.

questions.prompt: Founder of genetics whose pea-plant experiments produced 3:1 ratios.
questions.explanation: Gregor Mendel (1822-1884) was an Augustinian friar at St. Thomas's Abbey in Brünn (now Brno, Czech Republic) whose 1866 paper Experiments on Plant Hybridization founded genetics. He tracked seven discrete traits in 28,000 Pisum sativum (pea) plants, deriving the law of segregation, the law of independent assortment, and the dominant/recessive concept. The classic 3:1 phenotype ratio in heterozygous crosses (Tt x Tt) bears his name. His work was ignored until 1900 when Hugo de Vries, Carl Correns, and Erich von Tschermak independently rediscovered it. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Mendel for the rules of inheritance.

questions.prompt: Approximate percentage of energy passed between trophic levels in a food chain.
questions.explanation: The 10% rule states that only ~10% of energy at one trophic level is transferred to the next; the other 90% is lost as heat, used for metabolism, or unconsumed. Raymond Lindeman established this with his 1942 study of Cedar Bog Lake, Minnesota, founding ecosystem ecology. If grass stores 10,000 kcal, grasshoppers acquire ~1,000 kcal, frogs ~100 kcal, snakes ~10 kcal, and hawks ~1 kcal. This is why apex predators are rare and food chains seldom exceed 4-5 levels. The principle underlies ecological pyramids and explains why eating lower on the food chain feeds more people. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test the 10% rule with energy pyramids.

questions.prompt: Number of chromosomes in a human gamete.
questions.explanation: Human gametes (egg or sperm) contain 23 chromosomes—the haploid number (n)—because meiosis reduces the diploid 46 (2n) to 23 (n). Joe Hin Tjio and Albert Levan correctly counted human chromosomes in 1956; previously researchers had counted 48. Each parent contributes 23 chromosomes at fertilization, restoring 46. The 23 include 22 autosomes plus one sex chromosome (X or Y). Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome, identified by Jerome Lejeune in 1959) results from nondisjunction yielding 47 chromosomes. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require recognizing 23 as the human haploid number and 46 as diploid.

questions.prompt: Theory of moving lithospheric plates that explains earthquakes and volcanoes.
questions.explanation: Plate tectonics is the unifying theory stating that Earth's lithosphere is broken into ~15 major plates that move atop the plastic asthenosphere. The theory was assembled in the 1960s by John Tuzo Wilson, Harry Hess (seafloor spreading, 1962), Fred Vine and Drummond Matthews (magnetic stripe evidence, 1963), and J.T. Wilson's 1965 transform faults paper. It explains earthquakes (at plate boundaries), volcanoes (subduction and divergent zones), mountain building (continental collisions), and continental drift (Wegener's 1912 hypothesis) in one framework. Plates move 2-15 cm/year. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify plate tectonics as the unifying theory of Earth surface processes.

questions.prompt: Long-term temperature shift driven primarily by burning fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: Climate change is long-term changes in Earth's temperature and weather patterns, increasingly driven by human greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuels. Svante Arrhenius first calculated in 1896 that doubling atmospheric CO2 would warm Earth by ~5-6 degrees Celsius. The IPCC (founded 1988) is the leading scientific authority. Earth's average surface temperature has risen ~1.1 degrees Celsius since 1880 per NASA GISTEMP, with the warmest 10 years all occurring since 2010. CO2 has risen from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to over 420 ppm in 2023—highest in 800,000+ years (Antarctic ice cores). NYS Grade 7 Science exams link fossil fuels and CO2 to global warming.

questions.prompt: Sedimentary rock principle that older layers lie below younger ones.
questions.explanation: The Law of Superposition states that in undisturbed sedimentary rock sequences, older layers (strata) lie below younger ones. Danish naturalist Nicolas Steno formulated this in his 1669 De Solido (along with original horizontality and lateral continuity), founding stratigraphy. William Smith's 1815 geologic map of England applied superposition to construct the first historical rock-age sequence. The Grand Canyon's 40+ visible layers (oldest Vishnu Schist at bottom, ~1.7 billion years; youngest Kaibab Limestone on top, ~270 million years) demonstrate superposition spectacularly. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to order rock layers using this principle.

questions.prompt: Supercontinent that existed ~300 million years ago and broke into modern continents.
questions.explanation: Pangaea was a supercontinent assembled from all of Earth's landmasses, existing from ~335 to ~175 million years ago. Alfred Wegener coined the name in 1912 from Greek meaning all earth. Pangaea broke apart in the Jurassic into Laurasia (North America, Europe, Asia) in the north and Gondwana (South America, Africa, Antarctica, Australia, India) in the south. The Atlantic Ocean opened ~180 million years ago and continues widening ~2.5 cm/year. Evidence includes matching coastlines, identical fossils (Lystrosaurus, Glossopteris) on separated continents, and matching rock formations. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test Pangaea as the past supercontinent.

questions.prompt: Method that uses isotope decay rates to date rocks billions of years old.
questions.explanation: Radiometric dating measures decay of unstable isotopes to determine absolute rock ages. Ernest Rutherford and Frederick Soddy discovered radioactive decay in 1902; Arthur Holmes pioneered radiometric dating of rocks in 1913. Common systems: uranium-lead (U-238 half-life 4.47 billion years, for oldest rocks), potassium-argon, rubidium-strontium. Clair Patterson used U-Pb dating of the Canyon Diablo meteorite in 1956 to establish Earth's age at 4.55 billion years. The oldest known mineral grains—zircons from Jack Hills, Australia—are 4.4 billion years old (Wilde, Valley et al., 2001). NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast radiometric (billions of years) with carbon-14 dating (under 50,000 years).

questions.prompt: Smallest unit of an element that retains its chemical properties.
questions.explanation: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains chemical properties. Democritus first proposed atoms ~450 BCE; John Dalton's 1808 atomic theory established the modern foundation. Atoms consist of a nucleus (protons + neutrons, discovered by Rutherford 1911 and Chadwick 1932) surrounded by electrons (J.J. Thomson, 1897). The atomic number (proton count) defines the element. A typical atom is ~10^-10 m across; a teaspoon of solid contains ~10^23 atoms. Atoms combine via bonds to form molecules and compounds. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require atoms as elementary chemical units with protons, neutrons, and electrons.

questions.prompt: Law stating mass is neither created nor destroyed in a reaction.
questions.explanation: The Law of Conservation of Mass states that mass is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions—only rearranged. Antoine Lavoisier established this experimentally in 1772-1789 by weighing reactants and products in sealed vessels, founding modern chemistry. His 1772 experiment burning tin in a sealed flask showed total mass unchanged. The principle was extended by Einstein's 1905 E=mc^2 to mass-energy equivalence, where nuclear reactions convert tiny amounts of mass to energy. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require balanced equations demonstrating conservation: the same atoms appear on both sides.

questions.prompt: Russian chemist whose 1869 periodic table predicted undiscovered elements.
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev (1834-1907), at St. Petersburg University, published the first widely accepted periodic table in 1869, arranging 63 known elements by atomic mass into rows where columns shared properties. He famously left blank spaces for undiscovered elements—eka-aluminum (gallium, 1875), eka-boron (scandium, 1879), and eka-silicon (germanium, 1886)—and predicted their properties accurately. Henry Moseley's 1913 X-ray work reordered the table by atomic number. Element 101 was named mendelevium in 1955. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Mendeleev as the creator of the periodic table.

questions.prompt: Reaction that releases heat, like burning natural gas.
questions.explanation: An exothermic reaction releases energy (usually heat) to the surroundings; products have less chemical potential energy than reactants. Marcellin Berthelot coined the term in 1875. Examples include combustion (methane burning releases 891 kJ/mol), neutralization (acid + base), respiration in cells, and most explosions. Igniting magnesium in oxygen produces magnesium oxide and 1,204 kJ/mol of heat plus blinding white light. The energy diagram shows products at lower energy than reactants, with an activation-energy barrier between them. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast exothermic (releases heat, hand warmers) with endothermic (absorbs heat, cold packs).

questions.prompt: Substance made of two or more elements chemically bonded together.
questions.explanation: A compound is a pure substance formed from two or more different elements chemically bonded in fixed ratios. Joseph Louis Proust formulated the Law of Definite Proportions in 1799: pure water is always 11.2% hydrogen and 88.8% oxygen by mass regardless of source. Compounds have properties different from their constituent elements—sodium (explosive metal) + chlorine (poison gas) → sodium chloride (edible table salt). The 2023 PubChem database catalogs 113 million chemical compounds. NYS Grade 7 Science exams contrast compounds (chemically bonded, fixed ratio) with mixtures (physically combined, variable ratio).

questions.prompt: Force that pulls all objects with mass toward each other.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the attractive force between any two objects with mass. Isaac Newton's 1687 Law of Universal Gravitation states F = G*m1*m2/r^2, where G = 6.674e-11 N*m^2/kg^2 (measured by Henry Cavendish in 1798). Earth's surface gravitational acceleration g = 9.8 m/s^2 means a 1 kg object weighs 9.8 N. Einstein's 1915 General Relativity reinterpreted gravity as curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy. Gravity holds the Moon orbiting Earth, Earth orbiting the Sun, and binds galaxies. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify gravity as the force pulling toward Earth and link it to weight.

questions.prompt: Newton's second law equation F equals m times this.
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law states F = ma: net force on an object equals mass times acceleration. The law appears in his 1687 Principia Mathematica. Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity (speeding up, slowing, or changing direction): a = (vf-vi)/t, measured in m/s^2. Force is measured in newtons; 1 N = 1 kg*m/s^2. A 1,500 kg car decelerating from 30 m/s to 0 in 6 seconds experiences a = -5 m/s^2, requiring -7,500 N of force. A Tesla Model S Plaid accelerates 0-60 mph in 1.99 seconds (~13.5 m/s^2). NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to solve F=ma problems.

questions.prompt: Newton's first law principle that resists change in motion.
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia) states that an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion continues at constant velocity unless acted on by a net external force. Galileo Galilei first proposed inertia in 1632, contradicting Aristotle's view that motion required continuous force. Newton formalized it in 1687. Inertia is proportional to mass—a bowling ball has more inertia than a tennis ball. Seatbelts protect passengers because in a crash, the car decelerates but the passenger's inertia keeps them moving forward; Nils Bohlin's 1958 Volvo three-point belt has saved an estimated million lives. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test inertia with at-rest and moving objects.

questions.prompt: Force opposing motion between two surfaces in contact.
questions.explanation: Friction is the force that resists relative motion between surfaces in contact. Charles-Augustin de Coulomb developed laws of friction in 1781, identifying static (preventing motion start), kinetic (resisting moving object), and rolling. Friction depends on normal force and coefficient of friction (mu), which varies by surface pair—rubber on dry asphalt mu~0.9, ice on ice mu~0.01. Without friction, walking and driving would be impossible. Lubricants (oil, ball bearings) reduce friction—Daniel Bonn's 2014 study found Egyptian pyramid builders may have used water-lubricated wooden sledges. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test friction as a resistive force.

questions.prompt: Simple machine wrapped inclined plane around a cylinder.
questions.explanation: A screw is a simple machine consisting of an inclined plane wrapped around a cylindrical shaft. Archimedes designed the Archimedean screw around 250 BCE for irrigating fields and bilge pumping. Pitch (distance between threads) determines mechanical advantage—finer threads provide greater force multiplication but require more rotations. Joseph Whitworth standardized screw threads in 1841 (British Standard Whitworth) and Henry Maudslay's 1800 screw-cutting lathe enabled mass production. Examples: wood screws, jar lids, drill bits, propellers, vises. NYS Grade 7 Science exams identify screws as inclined-plane simple machines and force multipliers.

questions.prompt: Scientist who discovered DNA's structure with Francis Crick in 1953.
questions.explanation: James Watson, working at Cambridge's Cavendish Laboratory with Francis Crick, published The Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids in Nature on April 25, 1953, proposing DNA's double-helix structure. Their model relied on Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 X-ray diffraction image and Erwin Chargaff's base-pairing rules. Watson, Crick, and Maurice Wilkins shared the 1962 Nobel Prize; Franklin had died in 1958 and was ineligible. Watson later directed the Human Genome Project (1990-92) and Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory. The discovery launched molecular biology and the modern biotechnology industry. NYS Grade 7 Science exams credit Watson and Crick with the 1953 DNA model.

questions.prompt: Process by which organisms convert food and oxygen into energy.
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration is the metabolic process converting glucose and oxygen into ATP, water, and CO2 in the mitochondria: C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O + ~36 ATP. Hans Krebs identified the citric acid (Krebs) cycle in 1937, winning the 1953 Nobel Prize. Mitochondria—the powerhouses of the cell (Philip Siekevitz, 1957)—evolved from ancient bacteria via endosymbiosis (Lynn Margulis, 1967). Each cell contains hundreds to thousands of mitochondria producing roughly your own body weight in ATP daily. Respiration is the opposite of photosynthesis. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require the respiration equation.

questions.prompt: Group of populations of different species interacting in one area.
questions.explanation: A community is the assemblage of all interacting populations of different species in a given area; the term was used by Karl Mobius in 1877 (biocenosis for oyster-bed communities) and by Frederic Clements in his 1916 plant community theory. A pond community might include frogs, fish, dragonflies, cattails, algae, and bacteria. Communities are characterized by species richness (number of species) and evenness (relative abundance). Food webs, competition, predation, and symbiosis describe community interactions. NYS Grade 7 Science exams require students to identify community as the multi-species level between population and ecosystem.

questions.prompt: Vertical column on the periodic table sharing similar chemical properties.
questions.explanation: A group is a vertical column of the periodic table containing elements with the same number of valence electrons and therefore similar chemical properties. The periodic table has 18 groups. Group 1 (alkali metals: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) are soft, react violently with water; Group 17 (halogens: F, Cl, Br, I) form salts; Group 18 (noble gases: He, Ne, Ar, Kr) are largely unreactive. Mendeleev arranged elements in groups by similar properties in 1869, though he then had only 8 groups. Henry Moseley confirmed the structure with atomic numbers in 1913. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test group properties, especially alkali metals and halogens.

questions.prompt: Energy due to motion, calculated as one half times mass times velocity squared.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: KE = (1/2)mv^2. Gottfried Leibniz first formulated vis viva (living force) as mv^2 in 1686; Thomas Young coined the name kinetic energy in 1807. SI unit: joule (J). A 1,500 kg car at 30 m/s has KE = 675,000 J. KE quadruples when speed doubles, which is why doubling driving speed quadruples stopping distance—a key 1950s-era driver-safety insight. Cellular ATP releases ~30 kJ/mol of energy. NYS Grade 7 Science exams test KE = (1/2)mv^2 calculations and contrast kinetic with potential energy.
